sections.heading: Scope of application
sections.title: Financial institutions covered by cyber risk management guidelines
sections.summary: This section explicitly states that the guideline applies to all financial institutions licensed or authorised by the Bank of Mauritius. It specifies that the effective date is 29 May 2023. The section defines key terms such as 'financial institution' to include banks, non-bank deposit-taking institutions, payment service providers, and operators of payment systems, clearing, and settlement systems. It also provides detailed definitions for terms like 'cyber and technology event', 'cyber and technology incident', and 'data breach' to ensure clarity in implementation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes the scope of the guideline, defining which financial institutions are subject to cyber risk management requirements and specifying critical terms like 'data breach' that directly impact data transfer assessments. It sets the foundation for understanding what constitutes a cyber incident and breach in cross-border contexts.

requirements.text: This guideline applies to all financial institutions licensed or authorised by the Bank.
This guideline shall come into effect on 29 May 2023.

sections.source_quote: This guideline applies to all financial institutions licensed or authorised by the Bank. Effective date: This guideline shall come into effect on 29 May 2023.

sections.heading: Scope of application (cont.)
sections.title: Definitions of cyber and technology risk management terms
sections.summary: This section provides detailed definitions for terms critical to cyber risk management, including 'cyber and technology event', 'cyber and technology incident', 'data breach', and 'financial institution'. It clarifies the scope of these terms to ensure consistent application across financial institutions. The definitions include specific examples, such as data breaches involving unauthorized access or disclosure, and distinguish between cyber incidents and attacks.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions are essential for DTIAs as they clarify what constitutes a data breach and cyber incident, directly impacting how data transfers and storage are assessed for compliance with cross-border data protection requirements.

requirements.text: administrative account refers to any user account with full privileges and unrestricted access to an operating system, database, application, security appliance, or network device.
asset refers to something of either tangible or intangible value that is worth protecting, including people, information, infrastructure, finances and reputation.
black box testing refers to a testing approach in which the tester is unaware of the environment.
board means the board of directors of a financial institution except for branches of foreign banks where 'board' means the local advisory board/committee.
control functions mean those functions that have a responsibility independent from management to provide objective assessment, reporting and/or assurance.

critical systems, services, infrastructures and other assets include systems, services, infrastructures and other assets whose failure will cause significant disruption to the operations of the financial institution.
cyber refers to what is related to, within, or through the medium of the interconnected information infrastructure of interactions among persons, processes, data, and information systems.
cyber and technology event means any observable occurrence in an information system.
cyber and technology incident means an event, whether resulting from malicious activity or not, which adversely affects the security of an information system or violates security policies.
cyber-attack is the exploitation of a weakness with the intention of achieving an adverse effect on the information and communication technology environment.
cyber-attacker means a person carrying out a cyber-attack.
data breach means a compromise of security that leads to the accidental, unauthorised, unlawful destruction, theft, loss, alteration, unauthorised use or disclosure of or access to data.
defence-in-depth refers to security strategy integrating people, processes and technology to establish a variety of barriers across multiple layers and dimensions of the financial institution.
Denial of Service means prevention of authorised access to information or information systems or the delaying of information system operations and functions.
dual control means a security procedure involving two distinct individuals operating in concert.
encryption is the process of encoding information under different levels/standards/protocols of encryption.
endpoint refers to a remote computing device that communicates with a network to which it is connected.

financial institution refers to any bank, non-bank deposit taking institution, cash dealer or payment service provider licensed by the Bank, or any operator of a payment system, clearing system or settlement system authorised by the Bank.
grey box testing refers to a testing approach in which the tester has a limited knowledge of the environment and its credentials.
identity and access management encapsulates people, processes and technology to identify and manage the data used in an IT system to authenticate users and grant or deny access rights.
information asset refers to any piece of data, device or other component of the environment that supports information-related activities.

Information Security and Management System refers to a documented management system that consists of a set of security controls that protect the confidentiality, availability, and integrity of assets.
information system refers to the set of applications, services, information assets or other information-handling components which includes the operating environment and networks.
interference means any impairment to the confidentiality, integrity or availability of computer data, corrupting a computer system, or impairing connectivity, infrastructure or support.
log refers to the automatically produced and time-stamped documentation of events relevant to a particular system.
malware refers to software designed with malicious intent containing features or capabilities that can potentially cause harm directly or indirectly to financial institutions.
multi-factor authentication refers to the use of two or more factors to verify a user's identity.
network infrastructure comprises hardware and software, systems and devices which enable computing and communication between users, services, applications and processes.
recovery point objective refers to the point to which information used by an activity is to be restored to enable the activity to operate on resumption.
recovery time objective refers to the period of time following an incident within which a product or service or an activity is to be resumed or resources are to be recovered.
red team testing refers to a controlled attempt based on targeted threat intelligence to compromise the cyber resilience of a financial institution by simulating tactics of real-life threat actors.
response function refers to the development and implementation of the appropriate activities to act on a detected cyber event.
restoration is the process of copying backup data from secondary storage and restoring it to its primary location or a new location.
scenario-based testing refers to a test that assesses a financial institution's response, resumption and recovery plans in order to strengthen cyber resilience.
security zone refers to a logical segmented section of a network that contains computer or computer systems grouped in accordance with specific security requirements.
service accounts can be privileged local or domain accounts used by an application or service to interact with the operating system.
situational awareness means the ability to identify, process and comprehend the critical elements of information through a cyber threat intelligence process.
third-party service provider refers to an entity undertaking an activity on behalf of the financial institution or providing a service to the financial institution.
threat intelligence means threat information that has been aggregated, transformed, analysed, interpreted or enriched to provide necessary context for decision-making processes.
validation refers to the process of checking the accuracy and quality of source data before using, importing or processing data.
vulnerability refers to a weakness, susceptibility or flaw of an asset or control that can be exploited by one or more threats.

vulnerability assessment refers to the systematic examination of an information system to determine the adequacy of security measures, identify security deficiencies, and provide data for predicting effectiveness of proposed security measures.

sections.source_quote: administrative account refers to any user account with full privileges and unrestricted access to: (a) an operating system; (b) a database; (c) an application; (d) a security appliance; or (e) a network device.

sections.heading: The board and senior management must promote a strong culture of awareness of cyber and
sections.title: Board must promote cyber risk awareness culture
sections.summary: This section mandates that the board and senior management must actively foster a strong culture of awareness regarding cyber and technology risk management within the financial institution. It emphasizes the importance of embedding risk awareness at all levels to ensure proactive identification and mitigation of potential threats.
sections.dtia_summary: This requirement ensures that the institution's leadership is actively involved in cultivating a risk-aware culture, which is critical for identifying and addressing data transfer risks before they escalate into cross-border incidents.

requirements.text: The board and senior management must promote a strong culture of awareness of cyber and technology risk management throughout the institution.

sections.source_quote: The board and senior management must promote a strong culture of awareness of cyber and technology risk management throughout the institution.

sections.heading: Both the board and senior management must collectively possess adequate expertise and
sections.title: Permanent data irrecoverability assurance for terminated contracts
sections.summary: Financial institutions must obtain assurance from third-party service providers that all data, including backups, becomes permanently irrecoverable and inaccessible within a timely period after contract termination. This ensures data is no longer accessible post-termination, critical for data deletion compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: DTIAs must verify that third-party providers can securely delete data upon contract termination to prevent residual data access across borders.

requirements.text: Obtain assurance from third-party service provider that all data is rendered permanently irrecoverable and inaccessible after contract termination

sections.source_quote: assurance from the third-party service provider through relevant independent reports/certificates that all data of the financial institution (including any backup) is rendered permanently irrecoverable and inaccessible in a timely manner after termination of the contract;

sections.heading: The board shall comprise at least one independent or non-executive director with relevant
sections.title: Board must include cyber-experienced independent director
sections.summary: This section specifies that the board must include at least one independent or non-executive director with relevant experience in cyber/technology matters. For Domestic Systemically Important Banks (D-SIBs), the board must have at least two such directors. Branches of foreign banks with local advisory boards must ensure at least one board member has relevant cyber expertise.
sections.dtia_summary: This requirement ensures that the board has specialized knowledge to evaluate data transfer risks, particularly for institutions handling cross-border financial data.

requirements.text: The board shall comprise at least one independent or non-executive director with relevant experience in cyber/technology related matters.

The board of local banks categorised as Domestic Systemically Important Banks (D-SIBs) shall comprise at least two independent/non-executive directors with relevant experience in cyber/technology related matters.

Branches of foreign banks having a local advisory board/committee should ensure that the aforesaid board/committee include at least one member with relevant experience in cyber/technology related matters.

sections.source_quote: The board shall comprise at least one independent or non-executive director with relevant experience in cyber/technology related matters.

sections.heading: The board and senior management must ensure that a comprehensive technology strategy
sections.title: Business continuity planning for third-party services
sections.summary: Financial institutions must identify alternative solutions to maintain business continuity during and after transition phases when using third-party services. This includes contingency planning, data portability, and ensuring services remain operational despite third-party disruptions.
sections.dtia_summary: DTIAs must assess third-party service continuity plans to ensure data remains accessible during transitions, critical for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Identify alternative solutions to ensure business continuity throughout and after transition phases

sections.source_quote: identification of alternative solutions to allow for business continuity throughout and after the transition phase;

sections.heading: The board is responsible for:
sections.title: Board responsibilities for cyber and technology risk oversight
sections.summary: The board must annually review and approve technology strategy, cyber risk management frameworks, and related policies. It must approve a cyber risk appetite statement defining acceptable risk levels for business goals. The board must receive regular reports on cyber incidents, threat landscapes, audit findings, and risk management framework effectiveness. It must ensure a Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) is appointed with board approval and oversee cyber risk management through appropriate sub-committees and culture promotion.
sections.dtia_summary: Board oversight of cyber risk frameworks and incident reporting is critical for DTIAs to ensure robust cross-border data handling practices and timely threat response.

requirements.text: Review and approve at least annually the technology strategy, cyber and technology risk management strategy and framework and related policies
Provide guidance in developing and approving a cyber and technology risk appetite and tolerance statement
Receive regular and timely reports on material cyber incidents, threat landscape evolution, audit findings, and risk management framework effectiveness
Ensure cyber and technology risk management matters are adequately discussed at board and relevant sub-committee meetings
Ensure the financial institution appoints a Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) with board approval
Ensure appropriate oversight of cyber and technology risks by control functions and external auditors
Promote a strong culture for cyber and technology resilience

sections.source_quote: The board is responsible for: reviewing and approving at least annually or more frequently depending on the threat landscape, the technology strategy, the relevant cyber and technology risk management strategy and framework and related policies;

sections.heading: The board of local banks categorised as a D-SIB shall set up a distinct cyber and technology
sections.title: D-SIB banks must establish cyber risk sub-committee
sections.summary: D-SIB (Systemically Important Banks) must form a distinct cyber and technology risk sub-committee with at least two directors having relevant expertise. This sub-committee oversees technology strategy, risk management frameworks, and critical projects. It must receive regular reports on cyber matters and report quarterly to the board, ensuring timely board awareness of material developments.
sections.dtia_summary: D-SIB banks require specific cyber oversight structures for DTIAs to manage cross-border data risks and ensure robust incident reporting.

requirements.text: Set up a distinct cyber and technology risk sub-committee comprising at least two directors with relevant cyber/technology experience
Oversight of technology strategy, cyber and technology risk management strategy and framework
Oversight of implementation of cyber/technology-related critical projects
Receive regular reports on cyber/technology related matters from relevant stakeholders
Report quarterly to the Board on cyber/technology matters
Ensure the board is promptly apprised of material developments

sections.source_quote: The board of local banks categorised as a D-SIB shall set up a distinct cyber and technology risk sub-committee comprising at least two directors with relevant experience in cyber/technology related matters.

sections.heading: The board of financial institutions other than local banks categorised as a D-SIB may, at its
sections.title: Non-D-SIB banks may delegate cyber responsibilities
sections.summary: Financial institutions not categorized as D-SIBs may delegate cyber and technology risk responsibilities to the Risk Management Committee or an appropriate board sub-committee. These delegated bodies must submit quarterly reports to the board and ensure timely board awareness of material developments.
sections.dtia_summary: Non-D-SIB banks can delegate cyber oversight to other committees, but DTIAs must ensure these delegated bodies maintain adequate reporting and oversight for cross-border data risks.

requirements.text: May delegate the above roles and responsibilities to the Risk Management Committee or an appropriate sub-committee of the board
Submit quarterly reports to the board
Ensure the board is promptly apprised of material developments

sections.source_quote: The board of financial institutions other than local banks categorised as a D-SIB may, at its discretion, delegate the above roles and responsibilities to the Risk Management Committee or to an appropriate sub-committee of the board.

sections.heading: Senior management is responsible for:
sections.title: Senior management responsibilities for cyber risk
sections.summary: Senior management must implement technology strategy and cyber risk management frameworks within board-defined risk appetite. They must ensure risk levels stay within tolerance, define staff roles, develop monitoring metrics, review frameworks annually, analyze threats, assess risk management effectiveness, establish training programs, validate training, escalate material concerns to the board, ensure staff expertise, and collect cyber incident data.
sections.dtia_summary: Senior management's role in implementing cyber risk frameworks directly impacts DTIAs by ensuring data handling practices align with cross-border risk tolerance and incident response capabilities.

requirements.text: Effectively implement the technology strategy and cyber and technology risk management framework and strategy
Ensure the level of cyber and technology risk assumed is within the board-defined risk appetite and tolerance
Define roles and responsibilities of cyber and technology risk management staff
Develop key performance metrics and indicators to monitor cyber and technology risks
Review technology strategy, cyber and technology risk management framework and policies at least annually or more frequently based on threat landscape
Analyze financial institution's cybersecurity threats and developments for timely decision-making
Closely monitor threat landscape, trends, and market developments affecting cyber risk management
Assess cyber and technology risk management framework effectiveness at least annually or upon major changes
Establish and maintain a robust cyber and technology risk awareness and training program for staff and board members
Assess and validate effectiveness of training and awareness programs
Promptly escalate material concerns to the board and relevant sub-committees
Ensure staff with access to sensitive information have adequate skills, experience, and relevant training
Collect and analyze information on cyber incidents, vulnerabilities, and threat intelligence trends

sections.source_quote: Senior management is responsible for: the effective implementation of the technology strategy and of the cyber and technology risk management framework and strategy;

sections.heading: The CISO shall:
sections.title: CISO qualifications and independence
sections.summary: The CISO must be a senior officer, part of the second line of defense, independent of IT operations, possess sufficient cyber/technology risk management expertise and experience, have adequate resources including personnel, and maintain direct access to the board and designated sub-committees.
sections.dtia_summary: CISO independence and expertise are critical for DTIAs to ensure effective oversight of cross-border data handling and incident response.

requirements.text: Be a senior officer
Be in the second line of defence
Be independent of IT operations
Have adequate skill, expertise and experience in cyber/technology risk management
Have sufficient resources, including an adequate number of competent personnel
Have direct access to the board and to the designated sub-committee of the board

sections.source_quote: The CISO shall: be a senior officer; be in the second line of defence; be independent of IT operations;

sections.heading: The CISO shall be responsible for:
sections.title: CISO responsibilities for cyber risk management
sections.summary: The CISO must develop and implement cyber risk management frameworks, provide quarterly reports to the board on risk positions and metrics, keep the Chief Risk Officer informed, support board and senior management, produce policies and procedures, monitor compliance with policies, ensure third-party oversight, monitor third-party services, design contingency plans, implement cyber resilience measures, address cyber-related queries, investigate incidents, monitor threat landscape evolution, and develop security awareness training programs.
sections.dtia_summary: CISO responsibilities for framework implementation and incident response are vital for DTIAs to ensure robust cross-border data handling and timely breach mitigation.

requirements.text: Develop and implement the cyber and technology risk management framework

Provide quarterly reports to the board on cyber and technology risk position, including trends in key performance metrics, training assessment results, threat landscape developments, and findings from testing exercises
Keep the Chief Risk Officer well informed of cyber/technology risk-related issues
Provide support to the board, relevant sub-committees and senior management on cyber and technology risk management matters
Produce relevant policies and procedure manuals for cyber and technology risk management
Monitor compliance to cyber and technology risk policies and procedures by the financial institution and relevant third-party service providers
Ensure adequate cyber and technology oversight of third-party service providers
Monitor services provided by third-party service providers in relation to cyber and technology risk management
Design and update contingency plans for cyber and technology issues
Implement cyber resilience measures
Ensure an adequate process to report and address cyber-related queries from internal and external parties
Investigate cyber incidents
Ongoing monitoring of the evolution of the threat landscape including current and emerging risks
Develop relevant cyber and technology security awareness and training programs

sections.source_quote: The CISO shall be responsible for: the development and implementation of the cyber and technology risk management framework;

sections.heading: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may entrust the roles and responsibilities of the
sections.title: CISO roles for foreign bank branches: delegation to senior officers
sections.summary: Foreign bank branches and subsidiaries may delegate CISO roles to senior officers in the second line of defense, independent of IT operations. The designated officer must report to the group or regional CISO and leverage group resources. The board must ensure these arrangements meet guideline requirements and provide adequate oversight through the officer, senior management, and board.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires delegation of CISO responsibilities to non-IT senior officers with board oversight, impacting how data transfer and processing roles are structured across foreign bank entities.

requirements.text: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may entrust CISO roles to an appropriate senior officer in the second line of defense
The designated senior officer must report to the group or regional CISO
The designated senior officer may rely on resources of the group/regional office
The board must ensure arrangements address all guideline requirements
The board must ensure adequate oversight by the designated officer, senior management, and board

sections.source_quote: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may entrust the roles and responsibilities of the CISO to an appropriate senior officer in the second line of defense and independent of IT operations. The designated senior officer shall report to the group or regional CISO and may rely on the resources of the group/regional office. The board shall ensure that the arrangements in place address all the requirements of the guideline and that there is adequate oversight by the designated officer, the senior management and the board.

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall have a board-approved technology strategy which takes into
sections.title: Board-approved technology strategy for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must have a board-approved technology strategy that aligns with organizational objectives, incorporates input from all departments, and includes vision, technology roadmaps, project timelines, and budgetary details for short and long-term technology goals.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates a comprehensive technology strategy with clear timelines and budgets, which affects how financial institutions manage data transfers and processing across their operations.

requirements.text: Financial institutions shall have a board-approved technology strategy
The technology strategy must take into consideration the objectives of the entire organization
The technology strategy must incorporate input from all departments
The strategy must include vision for short-term (up to three years) and long-term technology goals
The strategy must detail technology roadmaps for short-term and long-term objectives
The strategy must specify timelines for critical projects
The strategy must include associated costs and budgets

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall have a board-approved technology strategy which takes into consideration the objectives of the entire organization and input from all departments and includes: their vision, including where they are currently and where they aspire to reach over the short term (up to three years) and long term with respect to technology; their technology road map detailing the goals and objectives and the steps to be taken to achieve their vision over short term and long-term horizon; timelines for critical projects; and the associated costs and budgets.

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall have a board-approved cyber and technology risk management
sections.title: Board-approved cyber and technology risk management framework
sections.summary: Financial institutions must have a board-approved cyber and technology risk management strategy, framework, and related policies to ensure effective risk mitigation across their operations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires a formal cyber and technology risk management framework approved by the board, which directly impacts how data is protected during transfers and processing.

requirements.text: Financial institutions shall have a board-approved cyber and technology risk management strategy, framework and related policies

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall have a board-approved cyber and technology risk management strategy, framework and related policies.

sections.heading: Financial institutions can opt to have a standalone strategy, framework and relevant policies
sections.title: Option for standalone or integrated cyber risk management framework
sections.summary: Financial institutions may choose to have a standalone cyber and technology risk management framework and policies or integrate them into their enterprise-wide risk management framework.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows flexibility in structuring cyber risk management frameworks, which affects how data transfers and processing are coordinated across financial institutions.

requirements.text: Financial institutions can opt to have a standalone strategy, framework and relevant policies for cyber and technology risk management
Financial institutions may include cyber and technology risk management in their enterprise-wide risk management framework

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions can opt to have a standalone strategy, framework and relevant policies for cyber and technology risk management framework or include same in their Enterprise-Wide Risk Management Framework.

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall refer to industry best practices including relevant industry standards
sections.title: Adoption of industry best practices for risk management
sections.summary: Financial institutions must incorporate industry best practices and relevant standards when designing their cyber and technology risk management frameworks, considering operational complexity, technology infrastructure, and inherent risks.
sections.dtia_summary: This section emphasizes the use of industry standards to enhance data security, which is critical for ensuring safe data transfers and processing in financial contexts.

requirements.text: Financial institutions shall refer to industry best practices including relevant industry standards
Financial institutions must implement the most effective solutions considering operational complexity, technology, and inherent risks

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall refer to industry best practices including relevant industry standards when designing their frameworks and implement the most effective solutions taking into consideration, inter alia, the complexity of their operations and services, the underlying technology and inherent risks.

sections.heading: The cyber and technology risk management framework of a financial institution should:
sections.title: Cyber and technology risk management framework requirements
sections.summary: Financial institutions must ensure their cyber and technology risk management frameworks align with business strategy, risk appetite, documentation of processes, integration with overall risk management, threat landscape considerations, third-party risk assessments, and clear role definitions for risk management.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies detailed requirements for cyber risk management frameworks, directly influencing how data is protected during transfers and processing across international boundaries.

requirements.text: The framework must take into consideration the business strategy and technology strategy as approved by the board and the requirements of the guideline
The framework must be consistent with the cyber and technology risk appetite and tolerance statement approved by the board
The framework must document the processes behind the determination of cyber and technology risk management objectives and risk tolerance
The framework must document policies, procedures and controls taking into consideration the requirements of this guideline
The framework must be consistent with the overall risk management framework of the financial institution
The framework must take into consideration the current and evolving threat landscape, operational complexity, technology, and risks from vendors, market participants, and service providers
The framework must clearly specify roles and responsibilities including accountability for decision-making in cyber and technology risk management
The framework must consider interconnections with third parties from a cyber and technology risk perspective
The framework must be communicated to relevant staff of the financial institution

sections.source_quote: The cyber and technology risk management framework of a financial institution should: take into consideration the business strategy and technology strategy as approved by the board and the requirements of the guideline; be consistent with the cyber and technology risk appetite and tolerance statement approved by the board; document the processes behind the determination of the cyber and technology risk management objectives and risk tolerance; document the policies, procedures and controls taking into consideration the requirements of this guideline; be consistent with the overall risk management framework of the financial institution; take into consideration the current and evolving threat landscape, the complexity of the operations, services and technology and risks posed by vendors, other market participants and service providers; clearly specify the roles and the responsibilities including accountability for decision-making in respect of identifying, mitigating and managing cyber and technology risks; consider the interconnections of the institution with third parties from a cyber and technology risk point of view; and be communicated to relevant staff of the financial institution.

sections.heading: The cyber and technology risk management strategy and framework, including relevant
sections.title: Annual review of cyber and technology risk management framework
sections.summary: Financial institutions must review and update their cyber and technology risk management strategy and framework annually or following significant changes in threats, information systems, or new services. This review must reflect evolving threat landscapes, threat intelligence, and new technologies. The framework must include relevant policies and procedures to ensure ongoing relevance and effectiveness.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires regular updates to the risk management framework to address changing threats and technologies, impacting how data transfers and processing are assessed for security and compliance.

requirements.text: Review and update cyber and technology risk management strategy and framework annually
Review and update framework upon major changes in threat landscape or information system
Ensure framework reflects current and evolving threat landscape, threat intelligence, and new services/technologies

sections.source_quote: The cyber and technology risk management strategy and framework, including relevant policies and procedures, shall be reviewed and updated annually, or upon major changes in the threat landscape or in the information system, to reflect the current and evolving threat landscape, threat intelligence and the introduction of new services, technologies and service providers.

sections.heading: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may adopt the cyber and technology strategy and
sections.title: Foreign bank branches may adopt parent bank's cyber strategy
sections.summary: Foreign bank branches and subsidiaries can adopt their parent bank's cyber and technology risk management strategy and framework, provided it aligns with the Guideline's requirements and their own technology and business strategies. This allows for consistency while ensuring compliance with local regulations.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows foreign banks to leverage parent bank frameworks but requires alignment with local requirements, affecting cross-border data transfer and processing security.

requirements.text: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may adopt cyber and technology strategy and risk management framework of their parent bank
Adopted framework must be in line with this Guideline's requirements
Adopted framework must align with the branch's technology and business strategies

sections.source_quote: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may adopt the cyber and technology strategy and risk management framework, including relevant policies of their parent bank provided that they are in line with the requirements of this Guideline and their technology and business strategies.

sections.heading: The compliance, risk and internal audit (including IT audit) functions shall have the necessary
sections.title: Compliance functions must have expertise for risk management
sections.summary: Compliance, risk, and internal audit functions (including IT audit) must have sufficient expertise and resources to provide independent and objective assurance on the cyber and technology risk management framework. This ensures the framework is robust and effectively monitored.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures that internal audit functions can validate the effectiveness of risk management practices, critical for assessing data transfer and processing security.

requirements.text: Compliance, risk and internal audit (including IT audit) functions shall have necessary expertise
Compliance, risk and internal audit functions shall have necessary resources
Compliance, risk and internal audit functions must provide independent and objective assurance on cyber and technology risk management framework

sections.source_quote: The compliance, risk and internal audit (including IT audit) functions shall have the necessary expertise and resources to provide independent and objective assurance on the cyber and technology risk management framework.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall:
sections.title: Financial institutions must identify and assess cyber risks
sections.summary: Financial institutions must implement a framework for identifying cyber and technology risks, classify all assets and data, establish criticality ratings, maintain detailed inventories, conduct regular risk assessments, and update these assessments when new technologies or threats emerge. This includes third-party service providers and network connections.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires comprehensive risk identification and assessment for all data flows, directly impacting how cross-border data transfers are evaluated for security and compliance.

requirements.text: Implement framework for identification of cyber and technology risks
Identify, classify and document all functions, roles, processes, assets (including third-party service providers), data, devices, components and connection points
Establish rating methodology for criticality and sensitivity of accounts, information assets and systems
Maintain inventory of individual/system accounts, cyber services, key roles, processes, information assets, third-party providers and interconnections with criticality ratings
Regularly conduct risk assessments considering critical services, roles, processes, information assets and connection points
Conduct risk assessments when new products, services, technologies, projects or connection points are implemented
Conduct risk assessments when new information affecting cyber risks is identified
Maintain up-to-date network system maps including servers, routers, wireless networks, security devices, interconnections, dependencies and data flows
Identify cyber and technology risks from or posed to interconnections and coordinate with relevant entities to improve resilience
Comprehensively document individual and system accounts to track access rights to information assets
Review and update all risk management elements regularly and as changes occur

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall: (i) implement an appropriate framework for the identification of cyber and technology risks; (ii) ensure that all functions, roles, processes, assets (including those involving third-party service providers) and any other data, device(s), component(s) or connection point(s) of the network system are duly identified, classified and documented; (iii) establish a rating methodology for determining the criticality and sensitivity of individual/system accounts, information assets and other information systems; (iv) maintain an inventory of all individual and system accounts (including privileged and remote access accounts), cyber/technology services, key roles, processes, information assets, third-party service providers and interconnections together with criticality rating; (v) regularly conduct risk assessments taking into account critical services, roles, processes (including those involving third-party service providers), information assets and connection points; (vi) conduct risk assessments when new products, services, technologies, projects or connection points are being implemented and when new information affecting the cyber and technology risks, including the threat landscape of the financial institution are identified; (vii) maintain an up-to-date and complete network system (including network resources) maps. The map should, inter alia, contain all servers, routers, wireless networks and security devices forming the network system, interconnections as well as all external connection points, dependencies, and data flows with its connected information assets (including those with third-party service providers); (viii) identify the cyber and technology risks that the institution bears from or poses to its interconnections and coordinate with relevant interconnected entities to, inter alia, work together to address such risks, with the objective of improving the overall resilience of all parties; (ix) comprehensively document all individual and system accounts, including privileged and remote access accounts, so that it can track all access rights to its information assets; and (x) review and update all of the above regularly and as and when changes occur in a timely manner such that the risk assessments remain accurate and up to date.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall, with respect to exchange of information with customers and third
sections.title: Secure customer data exchanges via APIs
sections.summary: Financial institutions must perform risk assessments to maintain confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data exchanged with customers and third parties via APIs. They must implement security features proportional to the sensitivity and business criticality of the data being exchanged.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires specific security measures for data exchanges with customers and third parties, directly impacting how data transfers are assessed for security and compliance.

requirements.text: Perform risk assessment to ensure confidentiality, integrity and availability of data exchanged with customers and third parties via APIs
Implement security features commensurate with sensitivity and business criticality of data exchanged

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall, with respect to exchange of information with customers and third parties: (i) perform a risk assessment to ensure confidentiality, integrity and availability requirements of data being exchanged are maintained while allowing customers and third parties to connect to its IT systems via APIs; and (ii) ensure implementation of security features commensurate with sensitivity and business criticality of the data being exchanged.

sections.heading: A financial institution is encouraged to:
sections.title: Encouraged automated tools for risk management
sections.summary: Financial institutions are encouraged to implement automated tools for asset inventory management, identity and access management, and risk monitoring to enhance the efficiency and accuracy of their cyber and technology risk assessments.
sections.dtia_summary: Encourages the use of automation to improve risk assessment processes, which can enhance the security and compliance of data transfers and processing.

requirements.text: Implement automated tools (e.g., centralised asset inventory management) to support classification of critical services, roles, processes and information assets
Implement automated tools (e.g., centralised identity and access management) to support identification and classification of roles, user profiles and credentials
Use automated feeds to identify emerging risks and update cyber and technology risk assessment framework

sections.source_quote: A financial institution is encouraged to: (i) implement automated tools (such as a centralised asset inventory management tool) to support the classification of critical services, roles, processes (including those involving third-party service providers), information assets and connection points; (ii) implement automated tools (such as a centralised identity and access management tool) to support the identification and classification of roles, user profiles and individual and system credentials; and (iii) make use of automated feeds in order to identify emerging risks and update its cyber and technology risk assessment framework.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall:
sections.title: Financial institutions must implement robust security controls for system continuity and data protection
sections.summary: Financial institutions must establish security controls ensuring information system continuity and availability, confidentiality, integrity, and availability of stored information. These controls must address logical, physical, people, and third-party security aspects while being commensurate with cyber risks and business goals. Regular assessments are required to maintain effectiveness against evolving threats.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates comprehensive security controls that directly impact data transfer safety by requiring robust system resilience and data protection measures applicable to cross-border financial data flows.

requirements.text: Put in place robust and effective security controls for information system continuity and availability
Ensure confidentiality, integrity and availability of information stored in information systems
Develop security controls addressing logical, physical, people and third-party security
Ensure security controls are commensurate with cyber risks and consistent with business goals
Regularly assess security controls for effectiveness across all assets
Ensure security controls remain relevant to evolving cyber threats
Consider international standards and best practices when developing security controls
Integrate security checks into systems from the earliest development stages
Regularly review information security management system through certification, audits or testing

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall: put in place a robust and effective set of security controls that will enable it to fulfil its security objectives, which should include ensuring: the continuity and availability of its information systems; and the confidentiality, integrity and availability of information stored in its information systems

sections.heading: A financial institution shall:
sections.title: Financial institutions must implement network security boundaries and segmentation
sections.summary: Financial institutions must establish secure network boundaries using technologies like firewalls and routers, implement network segmentation based on system sensitivity, define baseline security requirements for systems, use encrypted network protocols, ensure end-to-end encryption for electronic channels, and maintain network diagrams that describe hardware, applications and data flows. Regular scanning for rogue devices and access points is also required.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes critical network security requirements that directly affect data transfer safety by mandating secure network boundaries, segmentation, and encryption for cross-border financial data flows.

requirements.text: Set up secure network boundary using routers, firewalls or virtual private networks
Distinguish trusted and untrusted areas based on risk profile and criticality
Apply reasonable access criteria using least privilege principle
Implement network segmentation according to system sensitivity
Define baseline systems and security requirements for remote access
Use secured network protocols for confidentiality and integrity of information
Establish end-to-end encrypted connections between branches, customer devices and systems
Provide reliability, redundancy and non-repudiation for online financial systems
Limit system sessions after predefined inactivity period
Incorporate defence-in-depth security architecture with up-to-date network diagrams
Implement technical measures to prevent unauthorized code execution
Strictly control and monitor system configuration changes
Implement intrusion detection/prevention systems for attack blocking
Prevent non-controlled devices from connecting to internal network
Log and monitor internal network activities for inappropriate use
Scan network infrastructure regularly for rogue devices and access points
Prevent users from installing unauthorized applications
Segment network infrastructure with risk-appropriate security policies

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall: set up a secure boundary to protect its network infrastructure through the use of technologies such as, inter alia, a router, firewall or virtual private network.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall:
sections.title: Financial institutions must implement strict access controls and authentication
sections.summary: Financial institutions must restrict logical access to system resources based on need to know and least privilege, establish policies for access privilege management including annual reviews, implement strong security standards for APIs including key management, deploy dual control for critical systems, regularly review access privileges, block unauthorized access, monitor privileged access, restrict system administration privileges, and implement password and multi-factor authentication policies for critical systems.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates strict access controls that directly impact data transfer safety by requiring robust authentication mechanisms for cross-border financial data flows.

requirements.text: Identify and restrict logical access to system resources based on need to know
Establish policies for access privileges management including annual reviews
Implement strong security standards for API key management including lifecycle
Implement strong encryption for API data transmission
Document third-party API access including identity, data accessed and timing
Implement measures to revoke API keys for suspicious activities
Establish dual control for access to critical systems
Regularly review access to information systems for unnecessary rights
Block unauthorized access to systems
Monitor critical systems with limited and controlled access
Restrict system administration privileges to operational requirements
Regularly review all access privileges according to defined procedures
Establish procedures for allocating privileged access including on-demand basis
Maintain logs to identify privileged user activities and access
Monitor privileged access for anomalous behavior
Establish authentication policies compliant with industry requirements
Implement controls to prevent unauthorized privilege escalation
Track privileged users' actions to detect and prevent anomalous behavior
Implement encryption for data at rest and in transit for critical data
Ensure access rights of departing employees are promptly revoked
Require departing employees to return all information assets
Adjust employee access rights upon employment status changes
Establish password policy with one-time password for first logon
Implement minimum password length requirement
Require alphanumeric passwords
Establish password history and common password criteria
Require regular password changes
Implement credential requirements including multi-factor authentication for higher risk access
Implement multi-factor authentication for administrative accounts
Implement multi-factor authentication for critical systems accessing customer information
Implement multi-factor authentication for remote access
Ensure biometric data processing for authentication complies with Data Protection Act 2017

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall: identify and restrict logical access to its system resources to the minimum required for legitimate and approved work activities, according to the principle of need to know and least privilege

sections.heading: A financial institution shall establish an appropriate framework in respect of multi-factor
sections.title: Financial institutions must implement multi-factor authentication for high-risk transactions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must establish a framework for multi-factor authentication that considers transaction risk level, value and recurrence. Higher risk transactions require multi-factor authentication to enhance security for electronic delivery channels.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires multi-factor authentication for high-risk transactions, directly impacting data transfer safety by mandating enhanced authentication for sensitive financial data flows.

requirements.text: Establish multi-factor authentication framework for electronic delivery channels
Consider transaction risk level, value and recurrence in authentication requirements
Apply multi-factor authentication to higher risk transactions

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall establish an appropriate framework in respect of multi-factor authentication for electronic delivery channels. This shall amongst others take into consideration the nature and the level of risk involved in the transaction, the value of transaction and the recurrence of the transaction. Higher risk transactions shall be subject to multi-factor authentication.

sections.heading: A financial institution is encouraged to:
sections.title: Financial institutions are encouraged to automate access management processes
sections.summary: Financial institutions are encouraged to automate access account administration to disable accounts after predetermined periods, implement automated identity and access management tools for system synchronization, establish automatic notifications for access changes, and use one-time passwords for critical applications.
sections.dtia_summary: This section encourages automation of access management processes that could enhance data transfer safety through improved access control mechanisms for cross-border financial data flows.

requirements.text: Automate administration of information system access accounts to disable and delete accounts after predetermined periods
Implement automated identity and access management tools for system synchronization
Implement tools for automatic notifications upon access changes
Use one-time password for critical applications

sections.source_quote: A financial institution is encouraged to: automate the administration of information system access accounts to, inter alia, disable and/or delete disabled, temporary and emergency accounts after a predetermined period of time and to detect unauthorised access

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall:
sections.title: Financial institutions must implement cryptographic controls for data protection
sections.summary: Financial institutions must establish encryption requirements aligned with recognized standards, implement controls to prevent unauthorized access to encryption keys, and manage cryptographic materials through hardware security modules, virtual tools, cloud-based services or third-party providers with appropriate safeguards.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates cryptographic controls that directly impact data transfer safety by requiring secure encryption and key management for cross-border financial data flows.

requirements.text: Establish encryption requirements aligned with recognized standards
Ensure encryption level considers criticality of underlying data assets
Store encryption keys separately from virtual images and data
Implement controls to prevent unauthorized access to encryption keys
Specify separate policies for management and access to cryptographic materials
Manage cryptography through hardware security modules, virtual tools, cloud-based services or third-party providers
Ensure third-party providers have appropriate arrangements to secure cryptographic materials
Understand circumstances for third-party access to keys and cryptographic materials
Ensure segregation between access to keys and customer information within third-party providers
Control access to cryptographic keys for core banking services

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall: establish requirements with regard to data encryption and use encryption and general cryptographic controls in accordance with recognised standards and processes, which address issues such as algorithm, key length, and key generation, among others.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall:
sections.title: Physical security for financial data centers
sections.summary: Financial institutions must implement physical security measures to protect premises, sensitive areas, and data centers from unauthorized access and hazards. They must restrict physical access to information systems based on need-to-know and least privilege principles. Institutions must maintain access logs, ensure critical systems have additional physical controls like CCTV and alarms, and promptly adjust access rights when employee status changes. They also need to maintain an inventory of technology assets with criticality ratings and physical locations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates physical security controls critical for data center protection, which directly impacts cross-border data transfers by ensuring physical safeguards for sensitive data infrastructure.

requirements.text: Implement appropriate measures to protect premises, sensitive areas and data centres from unauthorised access and natural hazards
Restrict physical access to information systems to minimum required for legitimate work activities based on need to know and least privilege
Maintain logs to identify physical access to information systems
Ensure limited physical access to critical systems with additional controls like CCTV monitoring and alarms
Promptly cancel or adjust access rights upon employee status changes
Maintain inventory of technology assets including criticality rating and physical location

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall: (i) implement appropriate measures to protect its premises, sensitive areas and data centres from unauthorised access and from natural and other hazards; (ii) identify and restrict physical access to information systems to the minimum required for legitimate and approved work activities, according to the principle of need to know and least privilege; (iii) ensure that appropriate logs are maintained to identify physical access to information systems; (iv) ensure that there is limited physical access to critical systems, services, infrastructures and other assets and that same are duly logged. Critical systems, services, infrastructures and other assets should also have additional physical controls such as being monitored by CCTV cameras and being secured by an alarm. The monitoring and management of CCTV cameras should be outside the control of the IT department; (v) ensure that access rights are promptly cancelled or adjusted upon change in employment status of employees; and (vi) maintain an inventory of their technology assets including their criticality rating, physical location (for instance, servers and their respective locations).

sections.heading: A financial institution shall:
sections.title: Change and patch management for financial systems
sections.summary: Financial institutions must establish change management policies with prioritization criteria, backup systems, and rollback strategies for information asset changes. They must implement patch management processes to enforce security and functional patches within critical timeframes, ensure thorough testing before deployment, specify emergency modification review and authorization processes, and maintain recovery plans for system failures.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes critical change and patch management requirements that affect data transfer security, particularly for systems handling financial data across borders.

requirements.text: Have policies, procedures and controls for change management including criteria for prioritising and classifying changes
Ensure all related information is backed up and a roll-back strategy is created before implementing changes
Establish a patch management process to enforce relevant functional and non-functional patches within critical timeframes
Ensure patches and changes are thoroughly tested before deployment
Specify processes for reviewing, authorising and enforcing emergency modifications
Have appropriate recovery plans in place

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall: (i) have policies, procedures and controls in place for change management, including criteria for prioritising and classifying the changes; (ii) ensure that all related information is backed up and a roll-back strategy is created before implementing changes to information assets to ensure that they are evaluated, checked, reviewed and accepted; (iii) establish a patch management process to ensure that relevant functional and non-functional patches (e.g. corrections for security vulnerabilities and program bugs) are enforced within a period commensurate with the patches' criticality and the financial institution's information systems; (iv) ensure that patches and changes are thoroughly tested before deployment; (v) specify the processes for reviewing, authorising, and enforcing emergency modifications; and (vi) have in place appropriate recovery plans.

sections.heading: A financial institution is encouraged to consider using standardised configuration of IT
sections.title: Standardized IT configuration for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions are encouraged to adopt standardized IT configurations to streamline patch management processes and enhance system consistency and security.
sections.dtia_summary: This section encourages standardized IT configurations which can improve data transfer security by reducing vulnerabilities through consistent system configurations.

requirements.text: Consider using standardized configuration of IT resources to facilitate patch management

sections.source_quote: A financial institution is encouraged to consider using standardised configuration of IT resources to facilitate its patch management process.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall:
sections.title: Technology refresh for financial systems
sections.summary: Financial institutions must avoid outdated and unsupported hardware or software, develop technology refresh plans for replacing end-of-support technologies, conduct risk evaluations for continued use, and regularly scan outdated systems for vulnerabilities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses technology refresh requirements critical for maintaining secure data transfers by ensuring timely updates of systems handling financial data.

requirements.text: Avoid using outdated and unsupported hardware or software, including legacy technologies
Develop a technology refresh plan for replacing outdated and unsupported hardware or software
Undertake risk evaluation for outdated and unsupported hardware and software to determine risk and appropriate risk reduction mechanisms
Ensure risk evaluation and risk reduction plan are approved by board or designated sub-committee and senior management
Regularly scan outdated and unsupported hardware and software for potential vulnerabilities

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall: (i) avoid using outdated and unsupported hardware or software, including legacy technologies; (ii) develop a technology refresh plan for replacing outdated and unsupported hardware or software, including legacy technologies, that are reaching end of support (EOS); (iii) ensure that a risk evaluation for outdated and unsupported hardware and software, including legacy technologies, is duly undertaken to determine the risk of continuing to use them and the appropriate risk reduction mechanisms for maintaining the risk at an acceptable level. The risk evaluation and risk reduction plan should be duly approved by the board or the designated sub-committee of the board and senior management; and (iv) ensure that outdated and unsupported hardware or software, including legacy technologies are regularly scanned to identify potential vulnerabilities.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall ensure that:
sections.title: Staff competence and security for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must ensure staff, including contractors and service providers, are competent to handle technology risks, conduct appropriate background checks and fit and proper tests based on data criticality, and monitor physical and logical behavior patterns for anomalous activities in critical systems.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates staff security controls that are critical for data transfer integrity, particularly for personnel handling financial data across borders.

requirements.text: Ensure all staff, including contractors and service providers, are competent and skilled enough to conduct assigned roles and handle technology risks
Conduct appropriate background checks and fit and proper tests on staff, including contractors and service providers, commensurate with criticality of data and systems
Monitor physical and logical behavior patterns for critical information assets/systems to identify anomalous activities

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall ensure that: (i) all staff, including contractors and service providers, are competent and skilled enough to conduct the assigned roles and handle technology risks; (ii) appropriate background checks and fit and proper tests are conducted on staff, including contractors and service providers and their staff, commensurate with the criticality of the data and information systems to which they would have access; and (iii) physical and logical behaviour patterns (e.g. network use patterns and work hours, etc.) are monitored for critical information assets/systems to identify anomalous activities.

sections.heading: A financial institution is encouraged to implement innovative solutions (e.g. data analytics,
sections.title: Innovative threat detection for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions are encouraged to implement real-time threat detection solutions like data analytics, machine learning, and artificial intelligence to identify and respond to security incidents promptly.
sections.dtia_summary: This section encourages real-time threat detection capabilities that enhance data transfer security by enabling faster identification and response to cross-border data threats.

requirements.text: Implement innovative solutions (e.g. data analytics, machine learning and artificial intelligence) to detect and respond to threat activity in real time

sections.source_quote: A financial institution is encouraged to implement innovative solutions (e.g. data analytics, machine learning and artificial intelligence, etc.) to detect and respond to threat activity in real time.

sections.heading: The extent of due diligence to be performed, including the requirements for onsite audits or
sections.title: Due diligence scope based on service materiality and reliance level
sections.summary: Financial institutions must conduct due diligence on third-party service providers proportional to the materiality of services and IT assets, and the level of reliance on the provider for security controls. The scope must consider the provider's risk management systems, information security capabilities, and vulnerability assessments. This applies to all third-party service providers handling financial institution data.
sections.dtia_summary: DTIAs must assess the proportionality of due diligence based on service materiality and institutional reliance to ensure adequate security controls for cross-border data transfers involving third parties.

requirements.text: Due diligence scope must be commensurate with service materiality and IT asset importance
Due diligence must consider level of reliance on third-party provider for security controls

sections.source_quote: The extent of due diligence to be performed, including the requirements for onsite audits or remote audits, shall be commensurate with materiality of the services and of the IT assets involved and the level of reliance the financial institution places on the third-party service provider to maintain effective security controls.

sections.heading: The due diligence on a third-party service provider in respect of material services shall, inter
sections.title: Due diligence components for material third-party service providers
sections.summary: Financial institutions must evaluate third-party service providers for material services through specific criteria including risk management systems, compliance with regulations, financial resilience, data protection measures, and track record. This assessment must cover security controls, background screening, and recovery capabilities to ensure robust data handling.
sections.dtia_summary: DTIAs must verify that third-party providers meet stringent security and compliance standards before data transfers, including vulnerability assessments and financial resilience checks.

requirements.text: Assess adequacy of third-party's risk management and internal control systems
Evaluate third-party's compliance with data protection and information security regulations
Verify third-party's ability to maintain services during adverse conditions like cyberattacks
Confirm third-party's ability to recover systems within stipulated recovery time objectives
Ensure personnel with access to customer information undergo adequate background screening
Assess third-party's financial and operational resilience
Evaluate third-party's track record of at least three years for relevant services

sections.source_quote: The due diligence on a third-party service provider in respect of material services shall, inter alia, include: the adequacy of the third-party service provider's risk management and internal control systems, information security capabilities and security controls including the controls for protecting the confidentiality, integrity and availability of data taking into consideration the findings of vulnerabilities assessment, penetration testing, audit and/or other reviews provided by the third-party service provider, where relevant; the third-party service provider's compliance with the requirements of this Guideline, the applicable data protection, confidentiality and information security regulations or other legislations and adherence to international IT standards; the willingness and ability of the third-party service provider to service commitments even under adverse conditions, for instance, in the event of a cyber-attack or data theft; the ability of the third-party service providers to recover outsourced systems and IT services within the stipulated recovery time objective and recovery point objective; the verification of whether the personnel of the third-party service provider (including employees and subcontractors) with access to customer information are subject to adequate background screening, security training, access approvals and confidentiality arrangements; forward looking assessment of the financial and operational resilience of the third-party service provider; and an assessment of the proven track record of at least three years of the third-party service provider for such services.

sections.heading: With respect to third-party service providers having access to its information assets or hosting
sections.title: Obligations for third-party service providers with access to information assets
sections.summary: Financial institutions must ensure third-party service providers comply with all guideline requirements, undergo due diligence before onboarding, maintain updated inventories of critical third parties, and include specific contractual clauses. These clauses must cover compliance with Mauritian laws, data confidentiality, record-keeping obligations, and exit strategies to ensure continuity during transitions.
sections.dtia_summary: DTIAs must confirm contractual clauses ensure third-party providers meet local and international data protection standards, including exit strategies and data portability for cross-border transfers.

requirements.text: Ensure third-party service provider complies with all guideline requirements
Conduct due diligence before onboarding third-party service providers
Maintain updated inventory of third-party service providers with criticality ratings
Ensure contracts do not hinder Bank's supervisory powers
Include clauses ensuring continued effectiveness of cyber and technology risk management framework
Include clauses ensuring compliance with Mauritian data protection laws
Include confidentiality obligations aligned with Banking Act 2004
Include clauses ensuring compliance with Banking Act 2004 record-keeping obligations
Require third-party to provide notice of changes in subcontractors or data storage locations
Grant Bank right to audit third-party services remotely
Allow Bank access to third-party audit reports and test results
Ensure third-party provides data upon Bank's revocation of license
Allow financial institution to terminate agreements based on concerns about third-party
Require third-party to cooperate with Bank and provide required information
Include comprehensive exit strategies with regular reviews

sections.source_quote: With respect to third-party service providers having access to its information assets or hosting its information assets, a financial institution shall: ensure that the third-party service provider is complying with all requirements of the guideline; ensure that the necessary due diligence is carried out before onboarding the third-party service provider; be aware of the information security measures and controls established and ensure that they are in line with the requirements of the guideline; maintain an updated inventory of third-party service providers which have access to its information assets together with their criticality rating; ensure that the contracts with third-party service providers are duly executed, do not consist of any clause that would hinder the Bank from exercising its supervisory powers, and include relevant provisions: to ensure the continued effectiveness of its cyber and technology risk management framework; to ensure compliance with relevant laws in Mauritius (e.g. The Cybersecurity and Cybercrime Act 2021, Data Protection Act 2017, ICTA, etc.) or to relevant and equivalent laws at all times; to impose confidentiality obligations on the third-party service provider which are in line with the underlying objective of section 64 of the Banking Act 2004 or section 18 of the National Payment Systems Act 2018, as relevant; to ensure compliance with the record keeping obligations as set out in the Banking Act 2004; to create the obligation of the third-party service provider to provide reasonable notice in the event of changes in subcontractor or change in location where the data in its possession is stored or processed; on the right of audit (including remote audit) by the Bank, the financial institution, its external auditor, or any third-party service provider appointed by the Bank, the financial institution or its external auditor. The cost of audit by any third-party service provider appointed by the Bank shall be borne by the financial institution; on the right of access by the Bank, the financial institution or its external auditor to relevant audit reports/reports of other tests conducted by or arranged by the third-party service providers; on the right of the Bank or any third-party service provider appointed by the Bank to promptly take possession of all services and data relating to the financial institution in the event the Bank decides to revoke the licence of the financial institution or appoints a conservator. The procedures for executing the change of ownership request and for ensuring continuity of services during this process should be duly documented and agreed with the third-party service provider; on the right of the financial institution to terminate the agreement where, inter alia, the financial institution has concerns on the third-party service provider, the location of the data and the subcontractors involved; to ensure the obligation of the third-party service provider, where applicable, to cooperate with the Bank and provide access to information required by the Bank, the financial institution, its external auditor, or any third-party service provider appointed by the Bank; on exit strategies which are comprehensive and well documented. The exit strategies shall be regularly reviewed to ensure that they remain adequate and effective. The exit plan shall, as a minimum, include the following: incident management process, including the roles and responsibilities of each party; the governing law; and dispute resolution considering the chosen governing law for the contract.

sections.heading: assurance from the third-party service provider through relevant
sections.title: Permanent data irrecoverability assurance for terminated contracts
sections.summary: Financial institutions must obtain assurance from third-party service providers that all data, including backups, becomes permanently irrecoverable and inaccessible within a timely period after contract termination. This ensures data is no longer accessible post-termination, critical for data deletion compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: DTIAs must verify that third-party providers can securely delete data upon contract termination to prevent residual data access across borders.

requirements.text: Obtain assurance from third-party service provider that all data is rendered permanently irrecoverable and inaccessible after contract termination

sections.source_quote: assurance from the third-party service provider through relevant independent reports/certificates that all data of the financial institution (including any backup) is rendered permanently irrecoverable and inaccessible in a timely manner after termination of the contract;

sections.heading: identification of alternative solutions to allow for business continuity
