sections.title: Business continuity planning for third-party services
sections.summary: Financial institutions must identify alternative solutions to maintain business continuity during and after transition phases when using third-party services. This includes contingency planning, data portability, and ensuring services remain operational despite third-party disruptions.
sections.dtia_summary: DTIAs must assess third-party service continuity plans to ensure data remains accessible during transitions, critical for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Identify alternative solutions to ensure business continuity throughout and after transition phases

sections.source_quote: identification of alternative solutions to allow for business continuity throughout and after the transition phase;

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall ensure that third-party service providers involve in the conduct of
sections.title: Accreditation of third-party testing providers
sections.summary: Financial institutions must ensure that third-party service providers conducting independent audits, vulnerability assessments, penetration testing, and other testing exercises are accredited by a reputable body. This ensures third-party testing providers meet quality and security standards.
sections.dtia_summary: DTIAs must confirm third-party testing providers are accredited to ensure reliable security assessments for data transfers.

requirements.text: Ensure third-party service providers conducting independent audits and testing are accredited by a reputable body

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall ensure that third-party service providers involve in the conduct of independent audits, vulnerability assessments, penetration testing and other testing exercises are duly accredited by a reputed body.

sections.heading: A financial institution is encouraged to automate the process for detection and prevention of
sections.title: Automate detection of unauthorized third-party access
sections.summary: Financial institutions are encouraged to automate processes for detecting and preventing unauthorized access from third-party service providers. This encourages proactive security measures to identify and mitigate risks associated with third-party access to financial systems. The obligation applies to financial institutions handling customer data through third-party service providers.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to implement automated security monitoring for third-party access, which impacts data transfer and storage security assessments when using external service providers.

requirements.text: Automate the process for detection and prevention of unauthorized access from third-party service providers

sections.source_quote: A financial institution is encouraged to automate the process for detection and prevention of unauthorized access from third-party service providers.

sections.heading: No third-party service provider shall be responsible for the conduct of external independent
sections.title: Third-party service providers limited to 3-year external assessments
sections.summary: Third-party service providers cannot be responsible for external independent assessments, penetration testing, and vulnerability assessments for more than three consecutive years. This section sets a maximum duration for third-party service providers conducting security evaluations, ensuring periodic reassessment of security practices.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts third-party service providers from conducting security assessments for more than three years, affecting data transfer and storage security assessments when outsourcing security evaluations.

requirements.text: No third-party service provider shall be responsible for external independent assessments, penetration testing and vulnerability assessments for more than 3 years

sections.source_quote: No third-party service provider shall be responsible for the conduct of external independent assessments, penetration testing and vulnerability assessments for a continuous period of more than 3 years.

sections.heading: Where a third-party service provider has been responsible for the conduct of external
sections.title: Third-party service providers require 2-year cooling period after 3-year assessments
sections.summary: Third-party service providers who have conducted external independent assessments, penetration testing, and vulnerability assessments for three years or less must wait at least two years before being entrusted with similar tasks again. This ensures a cooling period to maintain security standards after periodic assessments.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires a 2-year cooling period for third-party service providers after conducting security assessments for three years or less, impacting data transfer and storage security assessments.

requirements.text: Where a third-party service provider has been responsible for external independent assessments, penetration testing and vulnerability assessments for 3 years or less, it shall not be entrusted with the aforementioned tasks before a period of 2 years from its last assignment

sections.source_quote: Where a third-party service provider has been responsible for the conduct of external independent assessments, penetration testing and vulnerability assessments for a continuous period of 3 years or less, that firm shall not be entrusted the responsibility of the aforementioned tasks before a period of 2 years from its last assignment.

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall be aware of the location (city and country) where their customer
sections.title: Customer data hosting location and jurisdiction requirements
sections.summary: Financial institutions must know the city and country where customer data and systems are hosted and ensure that foreign jurisdictions have data protection laws equivalent to Mauritian laws, allow for audits, and do not restrict access to data. They must also ensure contractual provisions for data disclosure to foreign authorities and termination rights if locations change.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to assess foreign hosting locations for data, ensuring equivalent data protection laws, audit rights, and contractual safeguards for data disclosures to foreign authorities.

requirements.text: Financial institutions shall be aware of the location (city and country) where customer and other material information assets/systems will be hosted
Service providers must advise instances where they were legally bound to disclose clients' data to foreign authorities in the past and potential future disclosures
Governing law and jurisdiction must be suitable for enforceability of contractual provisions in case of breach
Data protection laws in foreign jurisdictions must be in line with Mauritian data protection laws or equivalent
Foreign jurisdiction's laws must not restrict on-site examination audit and access rights of the Bank, financial institution, auditors or third parties
Foreign jurisdiction's laws must not restrict access to information by the Bank, financial institution, auditors or third parties
Contractual provisions must allow financial institutions to terminate agreements if there are concerns about new locations
Service providers must use reasonable efforts to notify financial institutions before disclosing data to foreign authorities
Service providers must implement legal and protective measures if unable to give prior notice due to legal or regulatory constraints
Financial institutions must be informed by service providers of any disclosures to foreign authorities

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall be aware of the location (city and country) where their customer and other material information assets/systems will be hosted and shall ensure that: (i) they consider the risk of foreign authorities having access to their data and require the service provider to advise of instances where it was legally bound to disclose clients' data to foreign authorities in the past and of any such potential disclosures in the future (if available) in their risk assessment; (ii) the governing law and jurisdiction chosen are suitable for enforceability of the contractual provisions in case of breach thereof on part of the service provider; (iii) data protection laws in the foreign jurisdiction where the data is hosted and processed are in line with the Mauritian data protection laws or data protection laws which are equivalent to the Mauritian data protection laws; (iv) the foreign jurisdiction's laws or regulations do not place any restrictions regarding: a. on-site examination audit and access rights of the Bank, the financial institution, its external auditors or any third party appointed by them; and b. access to the information by the Bank, the financial institution, its external auditors or any third party appointed by them; (v) there are appropriate contractual provisions allowing the financial institution to terminate the agreement in case there is a change in the location where they have concerns with the new location; (vi) the foreign authorities do not have access to the data of the financial institution.

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall ensure that the due diligence under sections 43 and 44 are conducted
sections.title: Due diligence for data hosting and security assessments
sections.summary: Financial institutions must ensure that due diligence under sections 43 and 44 is conducted by a competent officer or reputable firm. This ensures that security and data hosting assessments are carried out by qualified personnel or entities to maintain compliance with data protection standards.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to conduct due diligence on data hosting and security assessments by qualified personnel, impacting data transfer and storage security assessments.

requirements.text: Financial institutions shall ensure that due diligence under sections 43 and 44 are conducted by a competent officer of the financial institution or a reputed firm

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall ensure that the due diligence under sections 43 and 44 are conducted by a competent officer of the financial institution or a reputed firm, as deemed appropriate.

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall establish a pre-agreed list of locations where its data will be
sections.title: Pre-agreed data processing locations with service providers
sections.summary: Financial institutions must establish a pre-agreed list of locations where data will be processed with service providers. If data is processed outside this list, it must be limited to individual transactions, promptly informed about the location, and ensured to be securely processed and permanently removed from the location.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to pre-agree data processing locations with service providers, ensuring data is securely processed and removed from locations outside the agreed list.

requirements.text: Financial institutions shall establish a pre-agreed list of locations where data will be processed with the service provider
Data processed outside the pre-agreed list must involve only individual transactions initiated by end users
Financial institutions must be promptly informed by the service provider of the location where data is processed and the rationale
Service providers must ensure data is processed in a secured environment and permanently removed from the location within a reasonable timeframe

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall establish a pre-agreed list of locations where its data will be processed with the service provider. Where data is processed in a location outside the pre-agreed list, the financial institution shall ensure that: (i) such instances are limited to cases involving processing of individual transactions initiated by end users of the financial institution; (ii) it is promptly informed by the outsourced service provider of the location (city and country) where its data has been processed including the rationale thereof; and (iii) it obtains assurance from the service provider that the processing of data was done in a secured environment and that its data has been permanently removed from that location and transferred to a pre-agreed location within a reasonable timeframe.

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall implement secure coding, source code review and application
sections.title: Secure coding and application security testing for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must implement secure coding practices, source code reviews, and application security testing. Critical applications must comply with relevant international standards. These requirements apply to all financial institutions handling digital systems.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates specific security controls for critical applications, directly impacting how financial institutions assess and implement technical safeguards during data transfers and processing.

requirements.text: Financial institutions shall implement secure coding
Financial institutions shall implement source code review
Financial institutions shall implement application security testing
For critical applications, financial institutions shall abide with relevant international standards

sections.source_quote: 47. Financial institutions shall implement secure coding, source code review and application security testing. For critical applications, financial institutions shall abide with relevant international standards.

sections.heading: The standard of secure coding and source code review should encompass such areas as secure
sections.title: Secure coding standards for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must define secure coding standards that cover secure programming techniques, input validation, output encoding, access control, authentication, encryption, and error handling. These standards apply to all software development activities within financial institutions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies technical security requirements that must be integrated into software development processes, affecting how data is handled and transferred across systems.

requirements.text: The standard of secure coding and source code review should encompass secure programming techniques
The standard of secure coding and source code review should encompass input validation
The standard of secure coding and source code review should encompass output encoding
The standard of secure coding and source code review should encompass access control
The standard of secure coding and source code review should encompass authentication
The standard of secure coding and source code review should encompass encryption
The standard of secure coding and source code review should encompass errors and exceptions

sections.source_quote: 48. The standard of secure coding and source code review should encompass such areas as secure programming techniques, input validation, output encoding, access control, authentication, encryption, errors and exceptions.

sections.heading: A policy and procedure for using open-source software code and third-party software should
sections.title: Policy for open-source and third-party software usage
sections.summary: Financial institutions must develop a policy and procedure for using open-source and third-party software that requires these codes to be reviewed and tested before integration into their systems. This policy applies to all software development activities within financial institutions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates specific security controls for software integration, directly impacting how financial institutions manage third-party data transfers and system vulnerabilities.

requirements.text: A policy and procedure for using open-source software code and third-party software should be developed
The policy shall require that these codes are reviewed and tested before integration into the software/system of the financial institution

sections.source_quote: 49. A policy and procedure for using open-source software code and third-party software should be developed. The policy shall, inter alia, require that these codes are reviewed and tested before integration into the software/system of the financial institution.

sections.heading: The financial institution shall ensure that its software developers are qualified or have the
sections.title: Qualified software developers for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must ensure their software developers have the requisite knowledge and abilities to implement secure coding and application security standards while developing applications. This requirement applies to all software development activities within financial institutions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures that developers are technically competent to implement security measures, which is critical for maintaining secure data transfers and processing.

requirements.text: The financial institution shall ensure that its software developers are qualified or have the requisite knowledge and abilities to implement secure coding and application security standards while developing applications

sections.source_quote: 50. The financial institution shall ensure that its software developers are qualified or have the requisite knowledge and abilities to implement secure coding and application security standards while developing applications.

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall implement an appropriate risk management framework before
sections.title: Risk management framework for end-user applications
sections.summary: Financial institutions must implement a risk management framework before deploying applications developed by end users that are not maintained by their IT department. This framework must include adequate testing and approval processes for such applications.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires specific risk management controls for end-user applications, which directly impacts how financial institutions assess and manage data transfer risks from non-IT-developed applications.

requirements.text: Financial institutions shall implement an appropriate risk management framework before deploying applications developed by end users and which are not maintained by its IT department
This framework shall include adequate testing and approval

sections.source_quote: 51. Financial institutions shall implement an appropriate risk management framework before deploying applications developed by end users and which are not maintained by its IT department. This shall, among others, include adequate testing and approval.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall:
sections.title: Cyber and technology incident detection requirements
sections.summary: Financial institutions must monitor internal and external variables using signature and behavior-based detection mechanisms, establish baselines for user/device activities, actively monitor for phishing campaigns, implement real-time transaction monitoring, notify customers for high-risk transactions, monitor user activity for anomalies, regularly review detection capabilities, and employ a defense-in-depth strategy for incident detection.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates comprehensive incident detection mechanisms that directly impact how financial institutions identify and respond to data breaches and security incidents during cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Monitor internal and external variables by using a combination of signature monitoring for known vulnerabilities and behaviourally based detection mechanisms
Identify, consider and record the baseline profile of user/device activities based on the risk assessment conducted during the identification process to aid in detecting deviation from the baseline
Actively monitor for phishing campaigns targeting the financial institution or its customers
Establish a real-time monitoring system to detect suspicious or fraudulent transactions
Establish a system for notification of transactions above a certain limit and of higher risk transactions to their customers, including relevant details of the transaction
Monitor user activity, exceptions, cyber and technology risk events, connections, external service providers, devices and software and analyse same for unusual behaviour
Ensure that the effectiveness and appropriateness of its detection capabilities are regularly reviewed and enhanced and that related staff receive relevant training on the matter
Identify warning signs for detecting cyber and technology incidents and for determining if breaches have occurred
Employ a defence-in-depth strategy by implementing multi-layered detection controls that cover individuals, procedures, and technologies
Be able to track cyber and technology incidents and quickly change its security controls, working in cooperation with other stakeholders

sections.source_quote: 52. A financial institution shall: (i) monitor internal and external variables by using a combination of signature monitoring for known vulnerabilities and behaviourally based detection mechanisms; (ii) identify, consider and record the baseline profile of user/device activities based on the risk assessment conducted during the identification process to aid in detecting deviation from the baseline such as anomalous activities and events; (iii) actively monitor for phishing campaigns targeting the financial institution or its customers; (iv) with regard to electronic delivery channels: a. establish a real-time monitoring system to detect suspicious or fraudulent transactions; and b. establish a system for notification of transactions above a certain limit and of higher risk transactions to their customers, including relevant details of the transaction such as type of transaction, amount of the transaction and timing of the transaction; (v) monitor user activity, exceptions, cyber and technology risk events, connections, external service providers, devices and software and analyse same for unusual behaviour; (vi) ensure that the effectiveness and appropriateness of its detection capabilities are regularly reviewed and enhanced and that related staff receive relevant training on the matter; (vii) identify warning signs for detecting cyber and technology incidents and for determining if breaches have occurred; (viii) employ a defence-in-depth strategy by implementing multi-layered detection controls that cover individuals, procedures, and technologies, with each layer acting as a safety net for the layers before it; and (ix) be able to track cyber and technology incidents and quickly change its security controls, working in cooperation with other stakeholders.

sections.heading: A financial institution is encouraged to:
sections.title: Establish security operations center and event monitoring systems
sections.summary: Financial institutions must establish a Security Operations Centre or equivalent to monitor anomalous activities and events. They must also design automated systems like SIEM to centralize and analyze event information across sources. These systems must continuously track the risk environment to identify potential threats and vulnerabilities in real-time.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires robust monitoring and event analysis capabilities to detect threats early, which is critical for cross-border data transfers involving financial institutions. This section impacts DTIA by mandating real-time threat detection systems that must be operational globally.

requirements.text: Set up a Security Operations Centre or equivalent for tracking and detecting anomalous activities and events
Design and implement automated systems (e.g. SIEM) to capture, centralise, and compare event information from various sources
Log analysis must be conducted to constantly track the risk environment

sections.source_quote: A financial institution is encouraged to: set up a Security Operations Centre or equivalent for tracking and detecting anomalous activities and events; and design and implement automated systems (e.g. a security information and event management (SIEM) system) and/or relevant processes to capture, centralise, and compare event information from various sources, as well as log analysis, to constantly track the risk environment.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall have a board approved business continuity plan which, as a
sections.title: Board-approved business continuity plan with cyber incident recovery
sections.summary: Financial institutions must have a board-approved business continuity plan that identifies critical services, recovery objectives, and scenarios for cyber incidents. The plan must include business impact analyses, recovery strategies, third-party provider assessments, disaster recovery sites, and annual reviews to adapt to evolving threats. It must ensure continuity of critical operations during disruptions.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates comprehensive business continuity planning for cyber incidents, which is essential for DTIA as it ensures data availability and system resilience during cross-border disruptions. This section directly affects data storage and transfer continuity.

requirements.text: Identify critical services, key roles, processes, information assets and third-party service providers
Define recovery point objectives and recovery time objectives
Consider extreme but plausible scenarios of cyber and technology incidents
Include business impact analyses to assess potential impact quantitatively and qualitatively
Set out recovery and response plans to safeguard critical assets and maintain critical services
Ensure recovery and response team has necessary skills, experience and training
Define parameters for detecting cyber incidents that trigger recovery and response plans
Ensure third-party service providers' response and recovery plans are satisfactory
Consider continuity measures to mitigate third-party service provider failures
Develop, document and agree with third-party service providers on disaster recovery plans
Ensure third-party service providers' business continuity plans are regularly tested
Ensure third-party service providers have system resiliency and network redundancy
Set up disaster recovery sites geographically separated from primary sites
Ensure dual connectivity between main premises, disaster recovery sites and alternate data centres
Conduct ex-post investigation of cyber incidents and integrate findings into risk management framework
Review scenarios and business impact analyses annually to account for changes in threat landscape

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall have a board approved business continuity plan which, as a minimum: identifies its critical services, key roles, processes, information assets and third-party service providers; defines its recovery point objectives and recovery time objectives; considers extreme but plausible scenarios of cyber and technology incidents or other incidents which would impact its systems; includes business impact analyses to assess the potential impact of those scenarios quantitatively and qualitatively; sets out recovery and response plans, dependent on the various scenarios, to safeguard critical assets, maintain critical services and achieve its recovery objectives; ensures that its recovery and response team have the necessary skills, experience and training to address cyber or technology incidents; defines parameters regarding detection of cyber or technology incidents which would trigger its recovery and response plans; ensures that the response and recovery plans of third-party service providers are satisfactory; considers continuity measures to mitigate the impact of failures of third-party service providers.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall have a board approved response and recovery plan for cyber and
sections.title: Board-approved cyber incident response and recovery plan
sections.summary: Financial institutions must have a board-approved response and recovery plan for cyber incidents that identifies key stakeholders, defines roles and responsibilities, focuses on critical business operations, sets escalation criteria, conducts thorough investigations, and outlines immediate actions to contain incidents. The plan must also specify details for sharing information with stakeholders and regulators.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires specific incident response protocols that are critical for DTIA, especially when handling cross-border data transfers. This section ensures timely containment and recovery from incidents that could disrupt data flows.

requirements.text: Identify staff and external stakeholders essential to deal with cyber incidents and who would be alerted
Set out policies, procedures, roles and responsibilities to respond to and recover from cyber incidents
Focus on recovery of critical business operations to avoid adverse effects on operations and payment systems
Define criteria for prompt escalation of cyber incidents to the board or senior management
Conduct thorough investigation to determine incident nature and extent of damage
Take immediate actions to prevent further damage and contain cyber incidents
Identify affected systems, carry out malware analysis and check for attacker response
Include details of internal and external stakeholders, information to share and reporting timeframes

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall have a board approved response and recovery plan for cyber and technology incidents which, as a minimum: identifies staff and other external stakeholders who are essential to deal with such incidents and who would be alerted in case such an incident is detected; sets out the policies, procedures, roles and responsibilities to respond to and recover from cyber and technology incidents; focuses on recovery of critical business operations, supporting processes, information assets and their interdependencies to avoid adverse effects on its operations and on the financial system, including on payment systems and on payment service users, and to ensure execution of pending payment transactions; sets out the criteria for prompt escalation of cyber or technology incidents to the board or senior management based on the risk posed by the incident; ensures that, upon detection of an incident, a thorough investigation is conducted to determine the nature of the incident and the extent of the damage caused by the incident; sets out the immediate actions to be taken to prevent further damage and to contain a cyber or technology incident; ensures that, after the cyber or technology incident is contained, key components of the incident are eliminated through inter alia, identifying all affected systems in the financial institution so that they can be remediated, carrying out malware analysis and checking for any response from the attacker; includes details of internal and external stakeholders, including the Bank, the information that has to be shared and reported together with relevant timeframes, including in case of emergency.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall:
sections.title: Secure backup storage and restoration for critical data
sections.summary: Financial institutions must store backup information securely at alternate sites both online and offline. They must establish a backup strategy based on information criticality, ensure restoration with minimal downtime, test restoration procedures at least twice a year for critical information, and protect backup data confidentiality, integrity and availability during storage and transmission.
sections.dtia_summary: Directly impacts DTIA by requiring secure, geographically dispersed backup systems that must be maintained across borders to ensure data availability during disruptions.

requirements.text: Store backup information at an alternate site, both online and offline, safeguarded by protective and detective controls
Establish a system and data backup strategy with appropriate frequency based on criticality
Ensure restoration of system and data backups can be carried out with minimum downtime and limited disruption
Test restoration of system and data backups at least twice a year for critical information
Ensure backup data is protected at rest and in transit for confidentiality, integrity and availability

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall: store backup information at an alternate site, both online and offline, which should be safeguarded by protective and detective controls; establish a system and data backup strategy with appropriate frequency of backup which is based on the criticality of the information; ensure that the restoration of its system and data backups can be carried out with minimum downtime and limited disruption, in line with recovery objectives; test the restoration of its system and data backups to validate the effectiveness of its backup restoration procedures. The frequency shall be commensurate to the criticality of the information and be at least twice a year for critical information and systems; ensure that any backup data is protected at rest and in transit to ensure its confidentiality, integrity and availability.

sections.heading: With a view to facilitating forensic investigation, a financial institution shall:
sections.title: Forensic readiness for digital evidence
sections.summary: Financial institutions must consider threat scenarios to determine digital evidence collection needs, securely collect and store logs and evidence, develop retention policies for at least 7 years, train staff on evidence handling, ensure forensic expertise, and periodically review forensic readiness procedures.
sections.dtia_summary: Critical for DTIA as it ensures digital evidence is preserved and accessible for cross-border investigations, which is essential when data transfers involve international incidents.

requirements.text: Consider different threat scenarios and determine types of logs and digital evidence to collect
Ensure logs and digital evidence are collected, handled and stored securely and backed up
Develop an internal policy for retention of digital evidence with minimum 7-year storage
Train staff on responsibilities related to handling digital evidence
Ensure staff designated for forensic investigations have necessary knowledge and expertise
Periodically review forensic readiness procedures based on new knowledge and experience

sections.source_quote: With a view to facilitating forensic investigation, a financial institution shall: consider different threat scenarios and determine the types of logs and other pieces of digital evidence that should be collected; ensure that the logs and other digital evidence are collected, handled and stored securely and are duly backed up; develop an internal policy for the retention of digital evidence and logs. As a minimum, critical logs and digital evidence shall be stored for a minimum of 7 years; train its staff such that all those involved in an incident understand their responsibilities related to handling of digital evidence; ensure that staff specifically designated to conduct forensic investigation have the necessary knowledge and expertise to handle the digital evidence; periodically review its forensic readiness procedures in light of new knowledge and experience.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall test the adequacy of its information security controls through well-
sections.title: Regular testing of information security controls
sections.summary: Financial institutions must test the adequacy of their information security controls through well-documented assurance and testing programs. The frequency and nature of these tests must align with evolving threats, information asset criticality, incident consequences, risks of policy enforcement failures, and changes to information assets.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures that security controls are continuously validated, which is vital for DTIA to confirm that data transfers and processing meet security standards across borders.

requirements.text: Test the adequacy of information security controls through well-documented assurance and testing programs
Ensure testing frequency and nature align with evolution of threat landscape
Ensure testing frequency and nature align with criticality and sensitivity of information assets
Ensure testing frequency and nature align with consequences of information security incidents
Ensure testing frequency and nature align with risks associated with inability to enforce security policies
Ensure testing frequency and nature align with materiality and frequency of change to information assets

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall test the adequacy of its information security controls through well-documented assurance and testing programmes. The nature and frequency of the reviews and tests must be commensurate with: the evolution of the threat landscape; the criticality and sensitivity of the information asset; the consequences of an information security incident; the risks associated with exposure to environments where the regulated entity is unable to enforce its information security policies; and the materiality and frequency of change to information assets.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall ensure that the results of reviews, audit and testing exercises are
sections.title: Reporting of review and testing results to board
sections.summary: Financial institutions must report the outcomes of reviews, audits, and testing exercises to their board or designated sub-committees and senior management. This ensures oversight of internal risk management activities. The reporting requirement applies to all financial institutions operating under this guideline.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates that financial institutions report cyber and technology risk assessment results to governance bodies, which is critical for ensuring that data transfer and processing activities are subject to appropriate oversight and control mechanisms.

requirements.text: Financial institutions must report the results of reviews, audits, and testing exercises to the board or designated sub-committees of the board and senior management

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall ensure that the results of reviews, audit and testing exercises are duly reported to the board or designated sub-committees of the board and senior management.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall ensure that the assurance (excluding internal audit) and testing
sections.title: Annual review and approval of assurance and testing programmes
sections.summary: Financial institutions must have their assurance (excluding internal audit) and testing programmes reviewed and approved by the board or designated sub-committees at least annually or following material changes to information assets or business environment. The internal audit plan must be approved by the audit committee annually or after material changes. This ensures ongoing alignment with risk management frameworks.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires regular board-level approval of risk management frameworks, which impacts data transfer assessments by ensuring that third-party and cross-border data handling practices are continuously evaluated against evolving risks.

requirements.text: Financial institutions must have assurance (excluding internal audit) and testing programmes reviewed and approved by the board or designated sub-committees at least annually or when there is a material change to information assets or business environment
Internal audit plans must be approved by the audit committee at least annually or when there is a material change to information assets or business environment

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall ensure that the assurance (excluding internal audit) and testing programme (including the scope) are reviewed and approved by the board or designated sub-committees of the board at least annually or when there is a material change to information assets or the business environment. The internal audit plan shall be approved by the audit committee of the board at least annually or when there is a material change to information assets or the business environment.

sections.heading: The risk and internal audit (including IT audit) functions shall conduct periodic reviews of the
sections.title: Periodic reviews of cyber and technology risk management framework
sections.summary: Risk and internal audit functions must periodically review the cyber and technology risk management framework to ensure it aligns with the guideline and relevant standards, effectively mitigates risks, and that policies and procedures are being followed. This review process is critical for maintaining robust data protection practices.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires periodic validation of the risk management framework, which is essential for ensuring that data transfers and processing activities comply with regulatory requirements and mitigate cyber risks.

requirements.text: Risk and internal audit functions must conduct periodic reviews of the cyber and technology governance and risk management framework
Frameworks must be in line with the requirements of this guideline and relevant standards and in compliance with framework approved by the board and senior management
Frameworks must be effective and adequately mitigate the cyber and technology risks faced by the financial institution
All policies, procedures and processes must be duly being complied with

sections.source_quote: The risk and internal audit (including IT audit) functions shall conduct periodic reviews of the cyber and technology governance and risk management framework of the financial institution to, inter alia, ensure that: (i) the frameworks are: a. in line with the requirements of this guideline and relevant standards and in compliance with framework approved by the board and senior management; b. effective and adequately mitigate the cyber and technology risks faced by the financial institution; and (ii) all policies, procedures and processes are duly being complied with.

sections.heading: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may rely on reviews conducted by the compliance,
sections.title: Reliance on parent bank's reviews for foreign branches
sections.summary: Foreign bank branches and subsidiaries may rely on reviews conducted by their parent bank's compliance, risk, and internal audit functions, but must demonstrate appropriate oversight and control over the scope, frequency, findings, and risk mitigating plans of these reviews. This allows for resource sharing while maintaining accountability.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows foreign banks to leverage parent bank reviews for data transfer assessments, but requires demonstration of oversight to ensure that cross-border data handling meets regulatory standards.

requirements.text: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may rely on reviews conducted by the compliance, risk and internal audit functions of their parent bank
They should be able to demonstrate appropriate oversight and control on the scope, frequency, findings and risk mitigating plans of the reviews conducted by their parent bank

sections.source_quote: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may rely on reviews conducted by the compliance, risk and internal audit functions of their parent bank. However, they should be able to demonstrate appropriate oversight and control on the scope, frequency, findings and risk mitigating plans of the reviews conducted by their parent bank.

sections.heading: Financial institutions should have their governance and cyber and technology risk management
sections.title: External independent audit of governance frameworks
sections.summary: Financial institutions must have their governance and cyber and technology risk management frameworks audited by external independent assessors against the guideline and relevant standards on a periodic basis. The audit scope and frequency must be risk-based, with critical systems covered annually and all areas within two years for D-SIBs and three years for other institutions. Assessors must report to the board on framework effectiveness and maturity.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates external audits for risk management frameworks, which is crucial for ensuring that data transfers and processing activities are subject to independent validation and compliance with international standards.

requirements.text: Financial institutions should have their governance and cyber and technology risk management frameworks audited, against the requirements of the guideline and relevant industry standards, on a periodic basis by external independent, competent and reputed assessors
The scope and frequency of the independent assessment should be risk-based and include IT security audit
Critical systems, services, infrastructures and other assets as well as electronic delivery channels shall be covered annually
All areas shall be covered within a two-year cycle for D-SIBs and within a three-year cycle for other financial institutions
Assessors shall report to the board or the designated sub-committee of the board on the effectiveness of the cyber and technology risk management framework and its maturity model

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions should have their governance and cyber and technology risk management frameworks audited, against the requirements of the guideline and relevant industry standards, on a periodic basis, by external independent, competent and reputed assessors. The scope and frequency of the independent assessment should be risk-based and include IT security audit. Critical systems, services, infrastructures and other assets as well as electronic delivery channels shall be covered annually and all areas shall be covered within a two-year cycle for D-SIBs and within a three-year cycle for other financial institutions. The assessors shall report to the board or the designated sub-committee of the board on the effectiveness of the cyber and technology risk management framework as well as on its maturity model (e.g. an assessment of the cyber and technology controls, methods and processes against a clear set of external benchmarks).

sections.heading: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may rely on resources available at their parent bank
sections.title: Reliance on parent bank's resources for external audits
sections.summary: Foreign bank branches and subsidiaries may use resources available at their parent bank for conducting external independent audits, but must demonstrate the independence and competence of the respective auditors. This ensures that audit quality is maintained across the organization.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows foreign banks to share audit resources with parent banks, but requires verification of auditor independence and competence to ensure data transfer assessments meet regulatory standards.

requirements.text: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may rely on resources available at their parent bank for conducting the external independent audits
They should be able to demonstrate the independence and competence of the respective auditors

sections.source_quote: Branches and subsidiaries of foreign banks may rely on resources available at their parent bank for conducting the external independent audits. However, they should be able to demonstrate the independence and competence of the respective auditors.

sections.heading: In cases where the Bank finds it necessary, it may instruct a financial institution to undergo an
sections.title: Bank may require external cyber audit for financial institutions
sections.summary: The Bank of Mauritius may require financial institutions to undergo an external independent audit of their cyber and technology risk management governance, systems, and processes. The financial institution bears the cost of this assessment. This obligation applies to all financial institutions regulated by the Bank.
sections.dtia_summary: Financial institutions must undergo external audits for cyber risk management; this affects DTIA by requiring assessment of third-party service providers' security practices and audit quality.

requirements.text: The Bank may instruct a financial institution to undergo an external independent audit of its governance, systems and processes related to cyber and technology risk management
The cost of the assessment shall be borne by the financial institution

sections.source_quote: In cases where the Bank finds it necessary, it may instruct a financial institution to undergo an external independent audit of its governance, systems and processes related to cyber and technology risk management. The cost of the assessment shall be borne by the financial institution.

sections.heading: Financial institutions may, in the case of services provided by third-party service providers,
sections.title: Financial institutions may use third-party audit reports for risk management
sections.summary: Financial institutions can consider third-party service providers' audit reports if they are satisfied with the audit scope, reports, and the independence and competence of the auditors. This applies to services like information asset management where third-party providers conduct audits.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows financial institutions to use third-party audit reports for risk management, which impacts DTIA by requiring validation of audit quality and independence before relying on them.

requirements.text: Financial institutions may take into consideration reports of reviews, testing and other audits commissioned by third-party service providers
Where they are satisfied with the plan and scope of the audit/assessment and the reports
And the independence and competence of the auditors/assessors involved

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions may, in the case of services provided by third-party service providers, including the management of information assets, take into consideration reports of reviews, testing and other audits commissioned by the third-party service providers where they are satisfied with, inter alia, the plan and the scope of the audit/assessment and the reports; and the independence and competence of the auditors/assessors involved.

sections.heading: Financial Institutions shall ensure that they are duly apprised of the results of the exercise
sections.title: Financial institutions must be informed of third-party audit results
sections.summary: Financial institutions must be informed of the results of audits commissioned by third-party service providers and must be satisfied with the remediation plan to address identified vulnerabilities. This applies to all financial institutions using third-party service providers for information asset management.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to review and approve third-party audit remediation plans, which impacts DTIA by ensuring third-party service providers' security practices are validated.

requirements.text: Financial Institutions shall ensure that they are duly apprised of the results of the exercise commissioned by third-party service providers
And are satisfied with the remediation plan

sections.source_quote: Financial Institutions shall ensure that they are duly apprised of the results of the exercise commissioned by third-party service providers and are satisfied with the remediation plan.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall ensure that testing is conducted by appropriately skilled and
sections.title: Financial institutions must conduct security testing by skilled specialists
sections.summary: Financial institutions must ensure that security testing is conducted by appropriately skilled and functionally independent specialists, either internal or external. Testing includes vulnerability assessments, scenario-based testing, penetration testing, and red team testing to identify and address security vulnerabilities.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates specific security testing practices for financial institutions, which impacts DTIA by requiring detailed testing protocols for data security.

requirements.text: A financial institution shall ensure that testing is conducted by appropriately skilled and functionally independent specialists (internal to the financial institution or external specialists)
Testing include vulnerability assessment, scenario-based testing, penetration testing and red team testing

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall ensure that testing is conducted by appropriately skilled and functionally independent specialists (internal to the financial institution or external specialists). Testing include vulnerability assessment, scenario-based testing, penetration testing and red team testing.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall establish a process to conduct regular vulnerability assessment
sections.title: Financial institutions must conduct regular vulnerability assessments
sections.summary: Financial institutions must establish a process for regular vulnerability assessments on their information systems to identify security vulnerabilities and address risks in a timely manner. The frequency of assessments should be proportional to the criticality of the information system and the associated security risks.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires regular vulnerability assessments to identify and mitigate security risks, which impacts DTIA by ensuring timely identification of vulnerabilities in data systems.

requirements.text: A financial institution shall establish a process to conduct regular vulnerability assessment on their information systems to identify security vulnerabilities
And ensure risk arising from these gaps are addressed in a timely manner
The frequency of VA should be commensurate with the criticality of the information system and the security risk to which it is exposed

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall establish a process to conduct regular vulnerability assessment (VA) on their information systems to identify security vulnerabilities and ensure risk arising from these gaps are addressed in a timely manner. The frequency of VA should be commensurate with the criticality of the information system and the security risk to which it is exposed.

sections.heading: As a minimum, the critical systems, services, infrastructures and other assets of a financial
sections.title: Critical systems must undergo annual vulnerability assessments
sections.summary: Critical systems, services, infrastructures, and assets of financial institutions must undergo vulnerability assessments by independent specialists at least once a year to ensure timely identification and mitigation of security vulnerabilities.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates annual vulnerability assessments for critical systems, which impacts DTIA by requiring specific frequency and independence standards for security testing.

requirements.text: As a minimum, the critical systems, services, infrastructures and other assets of a financial institution shall be subject to VA by independent specialists on an annual basis

sections.source_quote: As a minimum, the critical systems, services, infrastructures and other assets of a financial institution shall be subject to VA by independent specialists on an annual basis.

sections.heading: The scope of the VA should, as a minimum, include vulnerability discovery, identification of
sections.title: Minimum scope for vulnerability assessment
sections.summary: This section mandates that the vulnerability assessment (VA) scope must include identifying weak security configurations, open network ports, and application vulnerabilities. It specifies that web-based systems require checks for common web-based vulnerabilities. The requirements apply to financial institutions conducting VA activities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines minimum technical vulnerabilities to assess for data security, directly impacting how financial institutions identify and mitigate risks before data transfers or processing.

requirements.text: The scope of the VA should include vulnerability discovery, identification of weak security configurations, and open network ports
The scope of VA should include application vulnerabilities
For web-based systems, the scope of VA should include checks on common web-based vulnerabilities

sections.source_quote: The scope of the VA should, as a minimum, include vulnerability discovery, identification of weak security configurations, and open network ports, as well as application vulnerabilities. For web-based systems, the scope of VA should include checks on common web-based vulnerabilities.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall perform VA before any deployment or redeployment of new or
sections.title: Mandatory vulnerability assessment before deployment
sections.summary: Financial institutions must perform vulnerability assessments before deploying or redeploying new or existing services that support major critical services, applications, and infrastructure components. The assessments must be conducted to fix bugs and weaknesses in line with established change and release management processes.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires vulnerability assessments before deploying critical systems, ensuring data security during transfers and processing activities.

requirements.text: A financial institution shall perform VA before any deployment or redeployment of new or existing services supporting major critical services, applications and infrastructure components
The VA must be conducted for fixing bugs and weaknesses consistently with change and release management processes

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall perform VA before any deployment or redeployment of new or existing services supporting major critical services, applications and infrastructure components for fixing bugs and weaknesses, consistently with change and release management processes in place.

sections.heading: A financial institution is encouraged to perform vulnerability scanning on an ongoing basis,
sections.title: Ongoing vulnerability scanning encouraged
sections.summary: Financial institutions are encouraged to conduct vulnerability scanning on an ongoing basis, rotating through different environments to ensure comprehensive coverage of all systems throughout the year. This practice helps maintain continuous security monitoring.
sections.dtia_summary: This section encourages regular vulnerability scanning to identify security gaps before data transfers or processing activities.

requirements.text: A financial institution is encouraged to perform vulnerability scanning on an ongoing basis
Rotating among environments to scan all environments throughout the year

sections.source_quote: A financial institution is encouraged to perform vulnerability scanning on an ongoing basis, rotating among environments in order to scan all environments throughout the year.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall carry out scenario-based testing exercises at least on an annual
sections.title: Annual scenario-based testing requirement
sections.summary: Financial institutions must conduct scenario-based testing exercises at least annually or after major changes in the threat landscape to validate response and recovery plans. The testing evaluates cyber threat and technology incident management capabilities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates regular scenario testing to ensure robust incident response, critical for data transfer security and resilience.

requirements.text: A financial institution shall carry out scenario-based testing exercises at least on an annual basis
Scenario-based testing must be conducted upon major changes in the threat landscape
The testing must validate response and recovery, as well as communication plans against cyber threats and technology incidents

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall carry out scenario-based testing exercises at least on an annual basis or upon major changes in the threat landscape to validate its response and recovery, as well as communication plans against cyber threats and technology incidents.

sections.heading: Depending on the exercise objectives, a financial institution shall involve relevant stakeholders,
sections.title: Stakeholder involvement in testing exercises
sections.summary: Financial institutions must involve relevant stakeholders, including senior management, business functions, corporate communications, crisis management teams, service providers, and technical staff responsible for cyber threat and technology incident detection, response, and recovery, based on the exercise objectives.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures comprehensive stakeholder engagement in security testing, critical for identifying data transfer risks and response capabilities.

requirements.text: Depending on the exercise objectives, a financial institution shall involve relevant stakeholders

Stakeholders include senior management, business functions, corporate communications, crisis management team, service providers, and technical staff responsible for cyber threat and technology incident detection, response and recovery

sections.source_quote: Depending on the exercise objectives, a financial institution shall involve relevant stakeholders, including senior management, business functions, corporate communications, crisis management team, service providers, and technical staff responsible for cyber threat and technology incident detection, response and recovery.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall carry out penetration testing to obtain an in-depth evaluation of its
sections.title: Penetration testing requirements
sections.summary: Financial institutions must conduct penetration testing to evaluate cyber and technology defences, using a combination of black box and grey box testing. The testing must cover both production and test environments to identify security weaknesses.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates penetration testing to identify vulnerabilities in data processing systems, directly impacting data transfer security.

requirements.text: A financial institution shall carry out penetration testing to obtain an in-depth evaluation of its cyber and technology defences
A combination of black box and grey box testing should be conducted
The penetration testing should include both production and test environments

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall carry out penetration testing to obtain an in-depth evaluation of its cyber and technology defences. A combination of black box and grey box testing should be conducted in this regard. The penetration testing should include both the production and test environment.

sections.heading: The frequency of penetration testing should be determined based on factors such as system
sections.title: Penetration testing frequency based on system criticality and risk exposure
sections.summary: This section mandates that penetration testing frequency is determined by system criticality and cyber risk exposure. Systems directly accessible from the internet or critical infrastructure require immediate testing post-deployment or after major changes, followed by annual testing. Independent specialists must conduct these tests.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires annual penetration testing for internet-accessible systems and critical infrastructure, impacting DTIA by mandating specific testing frequency and independence for high-risk systems.

requirements.text: Penetration testing frequency must be determined based on system criticality and cyber risk exposure
Independent specialists must conduct penetration testing for internet-accessible systems and critical infrastructure immediately after deployment
Penetration testing must be conducted for internet-accessible systems and critical infrastructure after major changes or updates
Penetration testing must be conducted annually for internet-accessible systems and critical infrastructure

sections.source_quote: The frequency of penetration testing should be determined based on factors such as system criticality and the system's exposure to cyber and technology risks. For systems that are directly accessible from the internet and for critical infrastructure, assets, systems, penetration testing shall be conducted by independent specialists immediately after deployment or whenever these systems undergo major changes or updates and thereafter on an annual basis.

sections.heading: D-SIBs shall perform red team testing to test and validate the effectiveness of their cyber and
sections.title: D-SIBs must conduct red team testing for cyber and technology defense validation
sections.summary: This section requires Designated Systemically Important Banks (D-SIBs) to perform red team testing to validate their cyber and technology defense and response plans against real cyber threats and incidents. The testing must assess the effectiveness of their security measures.
sections.dtia_summary: D-SIBs must validate their cyber defenses through red team testing, which impacts DTIA by requiring specific threat scenario testing for critical financial institutions.

requirements.text: D-SIBs shall perform red team testing to validate the effectiveness of their cyber and technology defense and response plan

sections.source_quote: D-SIBs shall perform red team testing to test and validate the effectiveness of their cyber and technology defence and response plan against prevalent cyber threats and technology incidents.

sections.heading: Other financial institutions are encouraged to perform red team testing.  Where the Bank finds
sections.title: Red team testing encouraged for other financial institutions with optional mandate
sections.summary: This section encourages other financial institutions to perform red team testing. The Bank of Mauritius may require such testing if deemed necessary, and the financial institution bears the cost of the testing activities.
sections.dtia_summary: Other financial institutions are encouraged to conduct red team testing, with the Bank having the authority to mandate it if necessary, impacting DTIA through testing requirements for financial data security.

requirements.text: Other financial institutions are encouraged to perform red team testing
The Bank may require a financial institution to conduct red team testing if necessary
The financial institution bears the cost of red team testing

sections.source_quote: Other financial institutions are encouraged to perform red team testing. Where the Bank finds it necessary, it may require a financial institution to conduct red team testing. The cost of the red team testing shall be borne by the financial institution.

sections.heading: The objectives, scope and rules of engagement should be defined before the commencement of
sections.title: Red team testing must be controlled and supervised to avoid system disruption
sections.summary: This section requires that the objectives, scope, and rules of engagement for red team testing be defined before commencement. The exercise must be conducted in a controlled manner under close supervision to prevent disruption to the financial institution's production systems.
sections.dtia_summary: Red team testing must be conducted in a controlled environment to avoid system disruption, impacting DTIA by requiring specific operational safeguards for testing activities.

requirements.text: Objectives, scope, and rules of engagement must be defined before red team testing commencement
Red team testing must be conducted in a controlled manner under close supervision
Red team activities must not disrupt the financial institution's production systems

sections.source_quote: The objectives, scope and rules of engagement should be defined before the commencement of the exercise and the exercise should be conducted in a controlled manner under close supervision to ensure the activities carried out by the red team do not disrupt the financial institution's production systems.

sections.heading: The threat scenario should be designed and based on challenging but plausible cyber and
sections.title: Threat scenarios must be challenging but plausible cyber incidents
sections.summary: This section mandates that threat scenarios for red team testing be designed to be challenging yet plausible cyber and technology incidents, reflecting real-life attacks to ensure the testing is relevant and effective.
sections.dtia_summary: Threat scenarios must be realistic and challenging, impacting DTIA by requiring specific incident scenarios for testing to validate security measures.

requirements.text: Threat scenarios must be designed based on challenging but plausible cyber and technology incidents
Threat scenarios must be based on real life attacks

sections.source_quote: The threat scenario should be designed and based on challenging but plausible cyber and technology incidents as well as real life attacks.

sections.heading: The financial institution shall design the exercise scenario by using threat intelligence that is
sections.title: Financial institutions must use threat intelligence for red team scenario design
sections.summary: This section requires financial institutions to design red team exercise scenarios using threat intelligence relevant to their specific threat environment to identify potential threat actors and tactics, techniques, and procedures likely to target them.
sections.dtia_summary: Financial institutions must use threat intelligence to design realistic red team scenarios, impacting DTIA by requiring threat-specific testing approaches for security validation.

requirements.text: Financial institutions shall design exercise scenarios using threat intelligence relevant to their threat environment
Financial institutions must identify threat actors most likely to pose a threat to the financial institution
Financial institutions must identify tactics, techniques, and procedures most likely to be used in attacks

sections.source_quote: The financial institution shall design the exercise scenario by using threat intelligence that is relevant to their threat environment to identify threat actors who are most likely to pose a threat to the financial institution and identify the tactics, techniques and procedures most likely to be used in such attacks.

sections.heading: The frequency of the different audits and testing exercises outlined above has been summarised
sections.title: Frequency of cyber security testing exercises
sections.summary: This section specifies minimum testing frequencies for various cyber security activities like vulnerability scanning, penetration testing, and external audits. Financial institutions must conduct these tests at defined intervals to ensure ongoing security of critical systems and infrastructure. The testing schedule varies by activity type and system criticality, with some requiring immediate action after deployment or major changes.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines minimum testing frequencies for cyber security activities that impact data transfer and processing security. Financial institutions must maintain specific testing schedules for critical systems to ensure adequate security during data transfers and processing.

requirements.text: Vulnerability scanning must be conducted immediately after deployment of critical systems
Penetration testing must be performed for critical systems and internet-accessible assets
External independent audit of governance and cyber risk frameworks must occur at least every 2 years for D-SIBS and 3 years for other financial institutions
Compliance, risk and internal audit reviews must occur at least every 3 years

sections.source_quote: Minimum testing frequency for vulnerability scanning: Immediately after deployment of critical systems, applications and infrastructure components.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall:
sections.title: Financial institutions must consider cyber threats and threat intelligence
sections.summary: Financial institutions must consider threats to data confidentiality, privacy, integrity and availability that could damage their reputation. They must include in threat analysis extreme but plausible cyber events, even if historically unlikely. The institution must analyze threat information with business data to anticipate cyber-attacks and share threat intelligence with relevant staff responsible for risk management frameworks.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to consider cyber threats and threat intelligence as part of their risk management, which impacts data transfer security and processing safeguards.

requirements.text: Financial institutions must consider threats to data confidentiality, privacy, integrity and availability that could damage reputation
Financial institutions must include in threat analysis extreme but plausible cyber events, even if historically unlikely
Financial institutions must analyze threat information with business data to anticipate cyber-attacks
Financial institutions must consider threat information from themselves, counterparties and other financial institutions
Financial institutions must make threat intelligence available to staff responsible for strategic cyber risk management

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall consider threats to confidentiality, protection, privacy, integrity and availability of its data and damage to its reputation that could result from such threats

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall establish a communication policy, including a trusted
sections.title: Financial institutions must establish communication policy for information sharing
sections.summary: Financial institutions must establish a communication policy that includes a trusted channel for timely information sharing with relevant stakeholders, including the Bank. This ensures effective coordination and timely dissemination of critical security information during cyber incidents or risk events.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to have a communication channel for sharing security information with stakeholders, which impacts data transfer and processing security.

requirements.text: Financial institutions must establish a communication policy including a trusted channel for timely information sharing with relevant stakeholders

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall establish a communication policy, including a trusted communication channel for timely information sharing with relevant stakeholders, including the Bank.

sections.heading: Financial institutions shall identify the types of information to be shared, as well as the
sections.title: Financial institutions must define information sharing conditions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must identify the types of information to be shared and the conditions under which information will be shared with relevant stakeholders. This ensures controlled and appropriate data sharing practices that align with security and privacy requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to define conditions for information sharing, which impacts data transfer security and privacy.

requirements.text: Financial institutions must identify types of information to be shared with relevant stakeholders
Financial institutions must define conditions under which information will be shared with relevant stakeholders

sections.source_quote: Financial institutions shall identify the types of information to be shared, as well as the conditions under which the information will be shared, with the relevant stakeholders.

sections.heading: A financial institution shall:
sections.title: Financial institutions must monitor and improve cyber risk management
sections.summary: Financial institutions must gather information on vulnerabilities and cyber incidents both internally and externally, including successful intrusions and near misses. They must analyze this information to determine impact on their risk management framework and review accordingly. Institutions must monitor technological developments to counter emerging threats and implement relevant measures. They must also circulate security materials to staff and monitor the effectiveness of their risk management framework.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to monitor and improve their cyber risk management framework, which impacts data transfer security and processing safeguards.

requirements.text: Financial institutions must gather information on vulnerabilities and cyber incidents both within and outside the institution
Financial institutions must analyze information to determine impact on cyber risk management framework and review accordingly
Financial institutions must monitor technological developments to counter emerging threats and implement relevant measures
Financial institutions must circulate relevant cyber security materials to staff when necessary
Financial institutions must implement and regularly monitor indicators to measure effectiveness of cyber risk management framework
Financial institutions must monitor progress in developing and enhancing cyber risk management framework to a more mature stage

sections.source_quote: A financial institution shall gather information on vulnerabilities and cyber incidents both within and outside the financial institution, including both successful intrusions and near misses

sections.heading: A financial institution is encouraged to analyse findings from audits, management information,
sections.title: Financial institutions encouraged to analyze audit findings
sections.summary: Financial institutions are encouraged to analyze findings from audits, management information, incidents, near misses, tests, exercises and external/internal intelligence to identify correlations that could improve their cyber and technology risk management framework.
sections.dtia_summary: Encourages financial institutions to analyze audit findings to improve their risk management framework, which impacts data transfer security and processing safeguards.

requirements.text: Financial institutions are encouraged to analyze findings from audits, management information, incidents, near misses, tests, exercises and external/internal intelligence

sections.source_quote: A financial institution is encouraged to analyse findings from audits, management information, incidents, near misses, tests, exercises and external/internal intelligence to find any correlation which could be used to improve its cyber and technology risk management framework.

sections.heading: A financial institution is encouraged to have a proactive approach to its cyber and technology
sections.title: Proactive cyber and technology security practices
sections.summary: Financial institutions are encouraged to adopt proactive measures for cyber and technology security by predicting future events through analysis of current information and trends. This approach emphasizes anticipating cyber threats and vulnerabilities to enhance organizational resilience. The guidance applies to all financial institutions operating within Mauritius.
